sources.title: Brod & Taylor - Tomato Pulp Crackers (drying method & yield)
Food Revolution Network - Dehydrated Juice Pulp Crackers

name: Iron Clay: Earthenware vs. Stoneware
teaser: Fire a ladder of tiles to find your clay's cone
hook: The same iron that stains a wild clay red is what caps how hot you can fire it: push an iron-rich terracotta past its range and it bloats, slumps, or turns to a black bubbled slag. Earthenware clays mature low (roughly cone 010-04); stonewares vitrify dense and waterproof higher (about cone 5-10). The only way to know which yours is, is to fire a ladder of test tiles and watch where it tightens up — and where it fails.

timeline.passive: Days of drying plus multiple firings

yield: This is a diagnostic card: a few pounds of clay yields a tile ladder plus a couple of trial pots. The payoff is knowing your clay's cone, not volume.

ingredients.amount: Enough for 6-10 test tiles + trial pots
ingredients.note: Red/brown color signals iron; iron is a flux that lowers the melting point.
A little talc or whiting can nudge a body; sand/grog opens it for thermal shock.

toolsRequired: Test kiln with pyrometric cones or accurate controller
Ruler + marker for shrinkage line (mark 10 cm)
Boiling setup for the fired-absorption test
toolsOptional: Kiln shelf posts to catch bloating/melting drips
steps: Make identical test tiles, each with a scored 10 cm reference line; record the wet length, then the bone-dry length to get drying shrinkage.
Fire tiles in steps across a range — e.g., cone 06, 04, 1, 5, 8 — placing each on a scrap shelf or in a catch-tray in case it melts.
After each firing, re-measure the line for fired shrinkage: shrinkage climbs to a maximum as the body densifies, then the body swells/bloats if overfired — that peak marks maturity.

Run the absorption test: weigh a fired tile, boil and soak it, weigh again; low absorption (under ~2%, ideally under 1.5%) means it is vitrifying like stoneware and is functionally watertight; 3%+ ware can still weep.

Watch for failure signs: blistering, warping, or slumping mean you have passed the body's ceiling — back off to the last good cone. Black coring is different: it usually means the firing climbed too fast for carbon and sulfur to burn out — slow the bisque and ensure good oxidation rather than lowering the cone.
Fire actual ware at that proven cone; glaze earthenware bodies to make them hold liquid, since they never fully vitrify.
modifiers: To extend an iron earthenware upward, blend in a more refractory light clay or reduce iron by blending. To lower a stubborn body, a touch of flux (talc, whiting, or feldspar) drops the maturing cone. Reduction firing deepens iron colors and can flux the body further; oxidation keeps brighter terracotta reds.
preservation: Unfired body stores wet and sealed. Correctly matured stoneware is waterproof and dishwasher-durable; earthenware stays porous and must be glaze-lined for utility. Overfired, bloated ware is scrap — recover only as grog.
beyondInk: Overfired, bloated, or melted tiles crush into grog and even into a raw slip glaze (some iron clays melt to a glossy brown 'slip glaze' at stoneware temperatures — the origin of Albany-type slips). Well-matured scraps become the sig and temper feedstock for other cards.
safety: Firing unknown wild clay can bloat or melt onto the shelf — always fire test tiles on a scrap shelf or catch-tray and vent the kiln (fumes from organics and sulfur).
Dry clay and any crushing of overfired scrap are silica hazards — process wet or masked.
Do not assume an iron wild-clay pot is food-safe; earthenware needs a tested food-safe glaze liner, and no raw or crazed surface should hold food.
Collect clay for food-contact ware away from roadsides, industrial fill, or contaminated ground, and use a lead-free, leach-tested liner glaze.
Occurrence is not permission — collect the clay only with consent.

sources.title: Ceramic Arts Network — A Simple Test Determines the Best Firing Temperature for a Clay Body
Digitalfire — Earthenware (glossary)
Linda Arbuckle — Earthenware Clays (handout)

name: Limestone & Marble as Whiting Flux
teaser: Grind the same rock as cliffs into a workhorse flux
hook: Limestone and marble are the same molecule — calcium carbonate — the metamorphic one just recrystallized. Ground fine it becomes 'whiting,' the most common calcium flux in ceramics: about 5% in a porous earthenware body helps counter the moisture expansion that makes glazes craze, and it is the backbone calcium of countless glazes. Fire it and the carbonate burns off as CO2 (the lime cycle in miniature), leaving reactive calcium oxide behind.
yield: A little goes far: hundreds of grams of whiting flux a large batch of glaze or body. Self-grinding limestone is laborious; this card is about a mapped source for a workhorse additive.

ingredients.item: Whiting (calcium carbonate) ground from mapped limestone or marble
ingredients.amount: ~5% as a body addition (much more as a glaze flux)
ingredients.note: Grind fine (~200 mesh); marble and limestone are both CaCO3. At low-fire temperatures whiting in the body works as a filler that suppresses moisture expansion and reduces fired shrinkage; its true flux action (hard, durable glass) applies to glazes at mid/high fire.
Any porous earthenware body prone to moisture-expansion crazing benefits.
Process wet to control dust.

toolsOptional: Acid-fizz test kit (vinegar) to confirm carbonate
Test kiln for glaze-fit ladders
steps: Confirm the stone is carbonate: a drop of vinegar on limestone or marble fizzes (CO2). That fizz is the working chemistry.
Crush and grind to a fine powder (~200 mesh), processing wet or masked — calcium carbonate dust is a nuisance/respiratory irritant and often carries free silica.
For a body addition, blend ~5% whiting into a porous earthenware body to reduce delayed moisture-expansion crazing.
For glaze use, weigh whiting as the calcium flux in a tested glaze recipe (calcium is a high-temperature flux giving hardness and durability).

Fire and note that carbonate decomposition starts around 1300-1500°F and is essentially complete by ~1650°F, releasing CO2 and leaving calcium oxide — vent the kiln and don't seal thick calcium layers that could bloat.
Test glaze fit on tiles; adjust whiting to tune the craze/shiver balance.
modifiers: As a glaze flux, more calcium = harder, more matte, more durable glass (to a point); too much crawls or matte-crazes. As a body addition keep it low (~5%). Dolomitic limestone adds magnesium (different, more matte effect). Calcined lime is caustic — see safety.
preservation: Dry whiting keeps indefinitely, sealed. In fired ware calcium is a permanent, durable flux. Beware 'lime pops': coarse limestone grains left in a body absorb moisture after firing, slake, and pop chips off the ware — grind fine to avoid this.
beyondInk: The same limestone/marble, burned hotter to quicklime and slaked, is the lime of lime plaster, limewash, and mortar — the full lime cycle. Marble also carves (the mineral-set carving card), and marble/limestone dust is a filler in gesso and clay-plaster.
safety: Grinding limestone/marble makes respirable dust that frequently contains free silica — grind wet, wear P100, never dry-sweep.
Calcining carbonate to quicklime (CaO) produces a strongly caustic material that burns skin, eyes, and lungs and reacts violently with water — do not attempt without proper training and PPE.
Coarse lime grains cause lime pops in fired ware — always grind fine.
Occurrence is not permission — collect mapped limestone/marble only with consent; glaze food-safety depends on a properly fluxed, tested glaze (never lead).

sources.title: Digitalfire — Calcium Carbonate (material)
Digitalfire — Glaze Crazing (troubleshooting)
Glazy — Major Oxides in Glazes

name: Silica & Quartz Body Opener
teaser: Fine silica that squeezes the glaze into fit
hook: Quartz is the quiet skeleton inside almost every clay body. Added as fine silica it fills the body, stiffens it against slumping, and — crucially — its own thermal expansion 'squeezes' the glaze on cooling to help keep it from crazing. Flint, chert, and Arkansas novaculite are all just microcrystalline SiO2; ground fine, they do the same job that bagged 'flint' does in a studio recipe.

timeline.passive: Days for wet processing and drying

yield: A small test body (e.g., 2 kg) uses only 100-500 g of fine silica; grinding your own is slow, so this card is about a targeted additive, not bulk production. Expect meaningful loss to oversize during screening.

ingredients.item: Mapped silica: chert / flint / quartz / novaculite (SiO2)
ingredients.note: Must be ground to a fine powder (target ~200 mesh) — coarse quartz causes dunting. Quartz is macrocrystalline SiO2; flint, chert, and novaculite are the microcrystalline forms — all behave as free crystalline silica in the body.

ingredients.item: Plastic clay body (wild or ball clay)
ingredients.note: Supplies plasticity and alumina the pure silica lacks.
Process everything wet to control silica dust.

toolsRequired: Jaw crusher or heavy mortar (calcining optional to ease crushing)
Ball mill or muller to grind fine
Eye protection and cut-resistant gloves for crushing
toolsOptional: Test kiln for fit tests
Dilatometer access (advanced) or simple craze/shiver test tiles
steps: Identify the silica: clear/white quartz, banded chert/flint, or Arkansas novaculite (whetstone-grade microcrystalline quartz) all work; keep it clean of contaminants. Chert, flint, and novaculite outcrops are often prehistoric quarry sites — if you see flaking debris, worked stone, or artifacts, do not collect.

Crush coarse, then grind to a fine powder. Conchoidal fracture of chert and flint throws razor-sharp flakes and flying chips during crushing — wear eye protection and cut-resistant gloves. Some potters heat (calcine) hard quartz to ~red heat and quench to make it crumble more easily — do this carefully and outdoors, mindful of steam bursts when quenching red-hot quartz.
Screen to ~200 mesh; discard oversize back into the crusher. Coarse quartz particles left in the body cause cracks (dunting) on cooling.
Do all crushing, grinding, and screening wet or inside a respirator — SiO2 dust is the classic silicosis hazard.
Blend the fine silica into the plastic clay: start around 10% by dry weight for a filler/opener, wedging or slip-mixing to full uniformity.
Make craze/shiver test tiles glazed with your standard glaze; adjust silica up (less crazing) or down (less shivering/dunting) to dial in glaze fit.
modifiers: As an 'opener' quartz reduces shrinkage and warping like grog but is finer. Line-blend 5/10/15/20/25% silica against a fixed glaze to map the craze-to-shiver window. Chert and flint behave like quartz; novaculite is prized for its extreme fineness and purity. Free quartz above ~25% risks cristobalite/dunting problems at stoneware temperatures.
preservation: Dry silica powder keeps indefinitely, sealed and dry (mask when opening). In the fired body silica is permanent; correct proportions give durable, craze-resistant ware, while excess can leave ware prone to cracking under thermal or mechanical stress.
beyondInk: The same fine silica is a glaze former (the primary glass-maker in most glazes) and a kiln-wash ingredient. Coarser rejected quartz becomes body temper or pit-fire aggregate. Novaculite's other life is as a whetstone — the sharpening card in the mineral set.
safety: Crystalline silica dust from quartz/chert/flint/novaculite is the leading silicosis hazard in ceramics — grind and screen wet, wear P100, and never dry-sweep.

Chert, flint, and novaculite outcrops are often prehistoric quarry sites — if you see flaking debris, worked stone, or artifacts, do not collect; disturbing such sites on federal or state land violates ARPA. Collect only raw, unworked float with landowner permission.
Calcining/quenching quartz throws hot fragments and can spall — do it outdoors with eye protection and heavy gloves.
Too-coarse or too-much free quartz causes dunting (cracked ware) and cristobalite conversion; keep it fine and moderate.
Occurrence is not permission — collect mapped silica only with landowner consent; leave protected outcrops untouched.

sources.title: Digitalfire — Silica / Quartz (material)
Digitalfire — Simple Physical Testing of Clays
Linda Arbuckle — Clays and Clay Bodies (handout)

name: Talc Low-Fire Body (from Soapstone)
teaser: Talc raises expansion so low-fire glazes fit
hook: Half the low-fire white clay in America is essentially talc and ball clay. Talc — the soft mineral that soapstone is mostly made of — raises a body's thermal expansion just enough to keep low-fire glazes from crazing, and a 50:50 talc-to-ball-clay slip is famously easy to cast with remarkably low shrinkage. But soapstone carries a warning glazed onto its own reputation: it can host tremolite asbestos, so any grinding of your own must be wet and respirated.

timeline.passive: Days drying; hazard-controlled grinding if self-milling talc

yield: A modest test body (2-4 kg) is plenty to prove the recipe. Self-grinding soapstone for talc is slow and hazardous; the honest recommendation is to buy milled talc and reserve mapped soapstone for carving.

ingredients.item: Talc (commercially milled ceramic talc strongly preferred over self-ground soapstone)
ingredients.note: Soapstone/steatite may contain asbestos and free quartz — buy asbestos-free ceramic talc unless you can verify (by laboratory PLM/TEM analysis, not appearance) and wet-grind a known-clean stone.
Supplies plasticity; a classic hobby casting body is 50 talc : 50 ball clay.
For slip casting; a plastic version uses just water.

toolsRequired: Test tiles + glaze for craze testing
toolsOptional: Wet lapidary/tile saw if cutting soapstone
Plaster casting molds
Dilatometer/craze-test setup
steps: Source talc safely: for anything you will handle as powder, use commercially milled, asbestos-free ceramic talc. Only consider self-processing soapstone if you can verify it is asbestos-free by laboratory analysis (PLM/TEM of a sample) — appearance or provenance alone cannot verify this. Vermont soapstones are often clean; New York and California stone are notorious for asbestos.
If cutting/grinding your own soapstone, do it wet with a respirator and HEPA control — never dry-grind steatite.
Weigh a starting body of 50% talc : 50% ball clay by dry weight (talc can range 40-60% depending on the effect you want).
For a casting slip, disperse with deflocculant and water; for a plastic body, wedge with just enough water to throw or hand-build.
Glaze test tiles and fire to cone 04 (talc bodies commonly mature cone 08-04); check that the raised body expansion has pulled the glaze into compression (no crazing).
Adjust talc up if glazes still craze, down if glazes shiver or the body gets too short/non-plastic.
modifiers: More talc = higher body expansion, better craze resistance, easier casting, lower shrinkage — but a shorter, less plastic body. Adding grog to a talc body improves thermal-shock resistance (a route to flameware). Talc also whitens the body. Do not fire talc bodies to stoneware temperatures — they are low-fire by design.
preservation: Dry talc keeps indefinitely, sealed and masked. Talc bodies dry and fire with notably low shrinkage, so they warp less; fired low-fire ware stays porous and needs glaze for utility. Cured plaster casting molds from these slips last many casts.
beyondInk: Talc is also a low-fire glaze flux (a magnesium/silica source giving matte, buttery surfaces). Soapstone's better craft life is as a carving stone and a heat-retaining griddle/bakeware stone — see the soapstone carving card in the mineral set.
safety: Soapstone/steatite and serpentine can contain tremolite asbestos and free quartz — never dry-cut or dry-grind; work wet with a P100 respirator and HEPA vacuum, or avoid self-processing and buy asbestos-free ceramic talc. Asbestos-free verification of raw soapstone requires laboratory analysis (PLM/TEM of a sample), not appearance or provenance alone.
Cosmetic/industrial talc can be quartz- or asbestos-contaminated; use ceramic-grade talc from a supplier who certifies asbestos-free.
Talc-body dust contains magnesium silicate plus crystalline silica from ball clay and possible quartz contamination — mask and wet-clean.
Occurrence is not permission — collect mapped soapstone only with consent; and low-fire ware needs a tested food-safe glaze for food use.

sources.title: Ceramic Arts Network — Techno File: Talc
Digitalfire — Talc (material)
Princeton EHS — Sculpture (stone dust / asbestos hazards)
USGS — Talc and Pyrophyllite (Mineral Commodity Summaries)

name: Tempered Wild-Clay Body
teaser: Open a sticky clay with sand or crushed grog
hook: The oldest fix in pottery is also the simplest: a too-sticky, over-shrinking clay is opened up by mixing in something already fired — grog — or clean sand. The ancients crushed old pots back into their bodies for exactly this. Temper reduces drying shrinkage and cracking (one test dropped drying shrinkage from 8% to 6.5% with 20% aggregate) and buys the thermal-shock resistance a pit or raku fire demands.

timeline.active: 1-2 hours mixing and wedging per batch
timeline.passive: Overnight+ to let the mix homogenize; days to dry ware

yield: Yield ≈ input clay plus the added temper weight; 80:10:10 turns 8 lb of clay into ~10 lb of body. Expect the tempered body to feel firmer and shrink noticeably less than the untempered clay.

ingredients.note: From the wild-clay processing card, at wedgeable stiffness.

ingredients.item: Clean silica sand (mapped silica/quartz, crushed & screened)
ingredients.note: Screen to a consistent mesh (~40-70). Prepare wet — silica dust causes silicosis.

Combine sand + grog to roughly 20% total aggregate for hand-building/pit firing. Crush only unglazed bisque or scrap from glazes you know are lead-free; never crush glazed ware of unknown origin — old/imported glazes may contain lead or barium. Never crush archaeological potsherds or artifacts (destroys the record and is illegal on public land under ARPA).

toolsOptional: Mortar & pestle or jaw-style crusher for making grog (from your own scrap bisque only)
Test-tile ruler
Pug mill for large batches
steps: Decide the aggregate load: ~10% for smoother throwing bodies, up to ~20-30% for hand-built or pit/raku work needing thermal-shock resistance and low shrinkage.

Make grog by crushing scrap bisque or old pots — only unglazed bisque or scrap from lead-free glazes, never glazed ware of unknown origin and never archaeological potsherds — then screen to size; prepare or buy clean silica sand and screen it too. Do all crushing/screening wet or masked — this is pure silica dust.
Weigh clay and aggregate. A common starting recipe is clay:sand:grog of 80:10:10 by weight (i.e., ~20% total temper).
Wedge the aggregate evenly into the plastic clay until no dry pockets or streaks remain; add a little water if the mix stiffens.
Rest the tempered body a day so moisture equalizes, then re-wedge before use.
Fire test tiles alongside your first pieces to confirm the temper hasn't pushed the body too porous or shifted its maturing cone.
modifiers: Coarser sand/grog = more open, tooth-y body good for large forms and thermal shock, but rougher to throw. Finer temper = smoother but less shrink relief. Line-blend temper at 3%, 7%, 15%, and 20% on test tiles to find your sweet spot. Grog adds thermal-shock resistance more than plain sand and shows as speckle in the fired body. Coarse quartz sand can microcrack (dunt) through quartz inversion at stoneware temperatures — prefer grog as the coarse fraction for high-fired or flame-facing ware.
preservation: Store sealed and moist like any clay body; tempered bodies age fine. Because temper opens the body, tempered ware dries more evenly and cracks less — but stays more porous, so it usually needs a glaze liner for any liquid-holding use.
beyondInk: Leftover screened sand and grog store indefinitely for the next batch or for a coarse pit-fire/raku body. Very coarse grog also makes a good kiln-shelf wadding and a non-slip additive for wedging bats.
safety: Silica sand, quartz, chert, and grog are all crystalline silica — crush and screen wet or with a P100 respirator; silicosis is cumulative and irreversible.
Never dry-sweep silica or grog dust; wet-mop or HEPA-vacuum.

Never crush glazed ware of unknown origin for grog (may contain lead or barium), and never crush archaeological potsherds or artifacts — that destroys the record and is illegal on public land (ARPA); use your own scrap bisque.
Occurrence is not permission — collect mapped silica/quartz only with consent and never from protected land.
A porous tempered body is not food-safe on its own; use a tested food-safe glaze liner for anything holding food or drink.

sources.title: Digitalfire — Grog (glossary)
Ancient Pottery — Tempering Pottery Clay: The Secret to Mixing Wild Clay
Linda Arbuckle — Clays and Clay Bodies (handout)

name: Wild Clay: Field Test & Processing
teaser: Bend a coil around your finger to test a clay bank
hook: Almost any exposed clay bank — a road cut, a creek meander, a builder's excavation — holds a clay that once made a local brick or roof tile. The test for whether it will hold a shape is beautifully simple: roll a coil and bend it around your finger. If it wraps without cracking, it's plastic enough to throw. Occurrence is not permission — a mapped deposit is not a dig site; get the landowner's or manager's yes before you take a bucket.

timeline.active: 3-5 hours across several days (digging, slaking, screening, wedging)

yield: A 2-gallon bucket of raw bank clay typically yields 8-15 lb of usable processed body after grit and water are removed — enough for a handful of small pots. Expect real losses to sand and stones; a sandy bank can lose half its volume.

ingredients.note: Occurrence is not permission — collect only with the landowner/land manager's consent, and never in parks or protected areas.
For slaking to a milk-thin slurry.

toolsRequired: Window screen + progressively finer sieves (window screen down to ~80-100 mesh)
Plaster bat or old bedsheet on plaster/wood for drying
toolsOptional: Ball mill or blender for a small test batch
Shrinkage ruler / test tiles
Test kiln access for fire tests
steps: Field test first: dig a walnut-sized lump, remove pebbles and roots, and roll a pencil-thick coil. Wrap it around your finger — no cracks means it is plastic enough to use; heavy cracking means it needs a more plastic clay blended in or is too sandy.
Dry the raw clay completely — bone-dry clay slakes fastest and most fully.
Slake: drop the dry lumps into a bucket of water (clay into water, not water onto clay) and let them collapse on their own for hours to a day. Stir to a smooth, milk-thin slurry.

Screen out roots and gravel through a window screen, then work down through finer sieves. Wear a mask whenever the material is dry — clay dust contains respirable crystalline silica, and chronic inhalation causes silicosis.
Levigate to drop sand: keep the slurry thin, let it stand ~10 minutes so sand settles, and pour or siphon the fine clay-in-water off the top, leaving the grit behind. Repeat for a smoother body.
Concentrate: let the poured-off slip settle for a day or more, siphon the clear water, then dry the thick slip on a plaster bat or a sheet over plaster until it reaches wedgeable stiffness.
Wedge thoroughly to remove air, then make test tiles: mark a 10 cm line on each, measure after bone-dry and again after bisque and glaze firings to learn the body's shrinkage and maturing cone.
modifiers: Too sticky (over-plastic): blend 10-30% fine sand or grog as temper (see the temper card). Too short or too sandy: levigate more and/or blend in a more plastic ball clay. White specks that fizz in vinegar are limestone — screen them out or fire higher; calcareous inclusions can cause lime-pops that spall fired ware weeks after firing. A magnet dragged through dry, screened clay pulls out coarse iron. Blend two wild clays to average out their behavior.
preservation: Store wet clay sealed in plastic to age — aging improves plasticity as bacteria develop. Dried, screened clay powder keeps indefinitely sealed and dry (mask when handling). Fire only after test tiles confirm the maturing range so ware neither stays porous nor bloats.
beyondInk: The levigated-out sand and grit is clean temper for the next batch or for a coarser pit-fire body. The finest suspended fraction, siphoned separately, is the starting stock for terra sigillata. Iron-stained wild clay can also be dried and ground as a slip or a raw glaze colorant.
safety: All clay dust is respirable crystalline silica — process wet whenever possible, wear N95/P100 when handling dry material, and never sweep dry (wet-mop or HEPA-vacuum).
Occurrence is not permission: a mapped deposit conveys no right to dig. Get explicit landowner or land-manager consent; digging is prohibited in most parks and protected lands.
Do not dig into stream banks or wetlands where it causes erosion or violates water rules; take small, backfill, and leave the bank stable.
White specks that fizz in vinegar are limestone — screen them out or fire higher; calcareous inclusions can cause lime-pops that spall fired ware weeks after firing.
Unknown wild clay can contain natural contaminants — never assume a raw wild-clay pot is food-safe until it is fully vitrified and, for food surfaces, lined with a tested, lead-free food-safe glaze.

sources.title: Digitalfire — How to Find and Test Your Own Native Clays
Ceramic Arts Network — Tips for Foraging and Processing Your Own Clay and Glaze
Digitalfire — Simple Physical Testing of Clays

name: Iron-Clay Celadon
teaser: Starve a whisper of iron of oxygen for jade-green glass
hook: Celadon is what happens when you starve a glaze of oxygen: a whisper of iron — under 2% — that would read yellow-brown in an ordinary fire instead turns translucent jade-green when the kiln burns reduction. Song-dynasty potters prized it as a stand-in for carved jade, and the color still comes from the humblest ingredients — feldspar, silica, limestone whiting, and a pinch of iron, some of it hiding in the wild clay itself. Get the iron and the atmosphere right and glass becomes stone-green light.

timeline.passive: A 10-14 hour reduction firing to cone 10 plus overnight cooling

yield: A 1000 g batch glazes ~18-22 pieces. Success hinges on the firing atmosphere more than the recipe — budget a couple of reduction firings to dial it in.

ingredients.note: Alkali flux; higher soda/potash pushes celadon bluer.
High silica supports the blue-green and glassiness.
Calcium flux for the classic transparent melt.
Alumina and suspension (as in the Alfred celadon). For the bluest celadons use a low-titania kaolin (Grolleg or NZ halloysite) instead of EPK; EPK's titanium shifts the color green-grey.
The colorant — keep low; more iron shifts toward olive then tenmoku.
A local red clay can supply some of both the clay and the iron — the mapped-material route.

toolsOptional: Oxygen probe
Ball mill
Test tiles
steps: Choose a tested base — e.g. Alfred celadon: silica 33 / whiting 20 / feldspar 27 / EPK 20, plus ~0.5-1% iron.
Weigh accurately; iron level is the whole game, so measure the colorant to a tenth of a gram.
Mix into water, sieve twice through 80-mesh for a flawless transparent film.
Glaze test tiles at two-three iron levels (0.6, 1, 2%) to bracket the green you want.
Fire to cone 10 in reduction: begin body reduction around cone 012-010 and hold moderate reduction to the end so the iron converts to its green ferrous state.
Cool and evaluate — oxidation flecks mean the reduction was too light or too late.
Check fit; add silica if it crazes. Blue celadons want high silica and low titanium.
Food-safe once fit is confirmed — the low iron does not leach.
modifiers: Iron drives color: ~0.5-1% = pale blue-green, ~2% = olive-green, 3-5% = amber/olive, ~8%+ = tenmoku black-brown. High soda + high silica + low titanium = bluer; more calcium and titanium = greener/greyer. A pinch of bone ash (1%) can add opalescence. Substitute a little wild red clay to source iron and clay from the map at once.
preservation: Store dry materials sealed. Mixed celadon keeps in a lidded bucket; re-sieve for a clean film. Fired ware is permanent and dishwasher-safe when well fitted.
beyondInk: The same base without iron is a clear high-fire liner glaze. High-iron variants of the recipe become tenmoku and iron-saturate glazes — the color family runs straight into oil-spot and kaki.
safety: Reduction firing produces carbon monoxide — fire gas/wood kilns with excellent ventilation and a CO detector.
Glaze mixing raises free silica from silica, feldspar, and clay — P100 respirator and wet-cleaning; silicosis risk.
Grind any foraged limestone/marble whiting or quartz WET and masked.
This glaze is food-safe by design (low iron, no toxic colorant) once fit is confirmed — but a crazed celadon should still get a liner or refit.
Handle iron oxide with a mask; the fine powder is a nuisance dust.

sources.title: Digitalfire — Celadon Glaze
Derek Au — Blue Celadon Glazes
Ceramic Arts Network — Celadons at Cone 6

name: Wood-Ash Glaze (40/40/20)
teaser: Rebuild the first accidental glaze from fireplace ash
hook: For a thousand years before anyone measured a glaze, the wood ash that drifted onto pots in the kiln melted into an accidental glass — the first glazes were pure serendipity. Sieve out your fireplace ash, blend it 40:40:20 with feldspar and clay, and you rebuild that ancient accident on purpose: the calcium and potash in the ash flux the silica into a runny, streaky celadon that pools amber in the throwing marks. No two batches match, because no two fires burn the same wood.

timeline.passive: 1-2 weeks to soak-wash ash, plus a cone 10 firing

yield: A 1000 g dry batch glazes roughly 15-25 mugs depending on thickness. Expect real variability batch-to-batch; ash glazes reward test tiles over hope.

ingredients.note: Unwashed ash keeps more soluble flux but is caustic and variable; washed ash is gentler and more repeatable. Hardwood (oak, ash, fruitwood) gives more flux than softwood.

ingredients.item: Potash or soda feldspar (e.g. Custer, Minspar)
ingredients.note: The alumina/silica backbone; substitute nepheline syenite to lower melt temp.
Adds alumina, suspends the slurry, and stiffens a too-runny melt.
Add to raise melting point and reduce running; helps glaze fit to cut crazing.
Deepens celadon green in reduction; keep low for classic pale celadon.

toolsOptional: Ball mill
Spray booth
Hydrometer
Test tiles
steps: Collect ash only from clean, untreated wood — never painted, pressure-treated, or glued scrap, which carry heavy metals and chlorides.
Wearing a P100 respirator and goggles, dry-sieve ash through 30-mesh outdoors or under local exhaust to remove charcoal and nails. Dry ash is caustic and lung-damaging.

Optional wash: soak ash in water in a lidded bucket for a week, pour off the caustic (soapy-feeling) water, refill, and repeat until the water runs clear and non-slippery. Wear gloves; the wash water can be as caustic as lye.
Weigh 40 ash : 40 feldspar : 20 clay by dry weight. Add colorant/silica per your line blend.
Mix into water to a thin-cream consistency, sieve through 80-mesh, and hydrometer to a repeatable specific gravity.
Glaze bisqueware thinly — ash glazes run. Leave the bottom 1/2 inch bare and stilt or wad if needed.
Fire to cone 9-10; in reduction the iron shifts to celadon green, in oxidation toward amber/tan.
Test every new wood source and wash-state on tiles before committing pots.
modifiers: Line-blend the ash:feldspar:silica triangle to tune the melt: more ash = runnier and glossier; more clay = stiffer and more matte; more silica = less running and better fit. Softwood ash and straw/grass ash carry more silica and phosphorus for opalescent 'oil-spot' effects. Layer thin over an iron slip for classic hare's-fur streaking.
preservation: Store dry ingredients sealed and labeled; store mixed glaze in a lidded bucket and re-sieve if it settles. Fired ware is permanent. Do not store wash water — neutralize and dispose per local rules; it is caustic.
beyondInk: Spent, neutralized ash-wash sludge can be composted in small amounts (it is essentially lime/potash). Sieved-out charcoal is fine for the garden. Leftover glaze slurry blends into other high-fire bases as a flux booster.
safety: Dry wood ash is CAUSTIC, and the dry glaze batch (flint, feldspar, clay) contains crystalline free silica — sieve and weigh outdoors or under exhaust wearing a P100 respirator, goggles, and gloves; silica dust causes irreversible silicosis.
Wash water and wet ash are strongly alkaline (lye-like) — wear chemical-resistant gloves and eye protection; can burn skin and eyes.
Never use ash from treated, painted, or manufactured wood — it concentrates arsenic, chromium, copper, and chlorides.
Runny ash glazes can weld pots to shelves — glaze thinly and protect kiln shelves.

Food-safety: a well-fitted ash glaze can be food-safe on liner surfaces only after per-batch testing for crazing and leaching (e.g., 24-hour lemon/vinegar test); high-iron or barium-bearing variants and crazed surfaces are decorative-only.

sources.title: Digitalfire — Wood Ash Glaze
Ceramic Arts Network — Using Wood Ash in Glazes
Digitalfire — Plant Ash Toxicity (hazard)

name: Limestone / Whiting Flux Glaze
teaser: The calcium flux behind nearly every stoneware glaze
hook: Whiting — the humble white powder in nearly every stoneware glaze — is just finely ground limestone or marble: calcium carbonate, the same rock that builds cliffs and cathedrals. Above cone 8 that calcium is one of the most reliable, durable fluxes in ceramics; below it, the same material can go stony and matte instead. Learning to place calcium in a glaze is learning to place the mapped limestone of your own region into the melt.
yield: A 1000 g batch glazes ~15-20 pieces. A reliable clear base you'll reuse as the parent for many colored variants.

ingredients.note: Higher amounts push toward calcia matte above cone 8; commercial whiting is purest, but clean marble/limestone can be milled fine.
Alkali flux + alumina/silica backbone.
Glass former; balances thermal expansion for fit.
Alumina for durability and suspension.
Opacifier for a cream/white matte.

toolsOptional: Ball mill
Hydrometer
Test tiles
steps: Weigh a balanced base — a classic Leach 4:3:2:1-style clear is ~feldspar 40 / silica 30 / whiting 20 / kaolin 10, adjustable. This specific base is a cone 9-10 recipe; cone 6 users should substitute part of the whiting with a calcium borate/boron frit (e.g., Ferro 3134) rather than firing it low.
If milling your own limestone or marble, cut and grind WET to avoid dust, then dry and screen to a fine flour. Respirator on for any dry handling.
Mix dry ingredients into water to a light cream, sieve twice through 80-mesh.
Hydrometer to a repeatable specific gravity and glaze test tiles.

Fire to the base's cone (9-10 for the Leach base above). Note that below ~cone 8 raw whiting may not fully melt and produces an immature, potentially soft/leachable satin or pinhole surface — this is not a finished food surface; fix by fritting some calcium.
Increase whiting toward 20-22% (with high firing) to intentionally make a durable calcium/lime matte; decrease toward 8-12% for a clean gloss.
Check fit for crazing; add silica to lower expansion if it crazes.
Leach-test any colored version — and any matte or underfired surface — before food use.
modifiers: This is the calcium-flux workhorse: 8-12% whiting reads glossy, 18-22% at cone 9-10 reads a true crystalline lime matte. Do not confuse that intentional matte with an underfired whiting surface below ~cone 8, which is immature and not food-safe. Swap part of the whiting for dolomite to add magnesium for a silkier matte. Add 1-3% iron for celadon, 0.5-2% copper (oxidation) for green — copper cautions apply to food surfaces.
preservation: Store dry ingredients sealed; keep milled limestone dry to prevent caking. Mixed glaze keeps for months to years if lidded but can grow mold or thicken; stir, re-sieve, and check specific gravity before reuse. Fired result is permanent.
beyondInk: Ground limestone/marble whiting is also the calcium source for glass, lime plaster, and a soil sweetener. Leftover clear base is the ideal liner glaze over decorative outer glazes.
safety: Grind limestone/marble WET and mask up — quartz/silica impurities and fine carbonate dust are respiratory hazards.
All glaze mixing raises silica dust from the silica, clay, and feldspar — P100 respirator, wet-clean, never dry-sweep.
Calcined lime (quicklime) is caustic; limestone glaze materials are not quicklime, but avoid heating raw limestone dust and skin contact with any lime.
Colored variants (copper, cobalt, manganese) can leach, and underfired/immature high-calcia surfaces should also be kept off food contact — use tested liners and leach-test before food use.
Zircon opacifiers are safe fired but the raw dust is a respiratory hazard; handle wet where possible.
Mapped limestone/marble occurrences are not permission to collect; rock/mineral collecting is prohibited in NPS units and regulated on most public lands — check land-manager rules first.

sources.title: Digitalfire — Calcium Carbonate (Whiting)
Ceramic Arts Network — Subbing Fluxes
Glazy — Major Oxides in Glazes

name: Raw Slip Decoration (Engobe)
teaser: Draw with the map itself — one clay on another
hook: The oldest decoration on pottery is just clay on clay: a slip — liquid clay — brushed, trailed, or combed onto a leather-hard pot so a light body shows through a dark one, or the reverse. It is the slipware of English country pottery and the mishima of Korea, and it needs no glaze chemistry at all, only a clay that shrinks at the same rate as the pot beneath it. Dig one clay for the body and another for the slip and you can draw with the map itself.
yield: A 1000 g dry batch of slip decorates dozens of pots — decoration uses little material. The limiting factor is fit, not quantity.

ingredients.item: Base clay (ball clay/kaolin or wild clay)
ingredients.note: Match the slip's shrinkage to the body; slips are higher-clay, engobes lower-clay with calcined additions.
Reduces shrinkage so the slip fits drier ware without cracking.
Adjusts shrinkage and hardness.
Flux for adhesion and to harden the slip at temperature.
Slips need more colorant than glazes, but rates differ by material: cobalt 1-4%, iron/copper ~8%, manganese 5-15%, commercial stains 10-15%.

toolsOptional: Rubber comb
Sgraffito tool
Bulb syringe
Banding wheel
steps: Mix a slip at 50-90% clay with water to a smooth pourable cream; sieve through 80-mesh. Wear a respirator while handling dry clay and oxides.
For color, blend in oxide or stain at the right rate for the material (cobalt 1-4%, iron/copper ~8%, manganese 5-15%, commercial stains 10-15%).
Test the slip's fit: brush it on a scrap of the same body at the same moisture and dry it — if it flakes or crawls, add calcined clay/silica to cut shrinkage.
Apply to WET-to-leather-hard ware for best bond (high-clay slips can crack on bone-dry pots). Brush, trail, dip, or pour.
Decorate: trail lines with a bulb, comb through for feathering, or carve back through for sgraffito.
Dry slowly and evenly to avoid the slip pulling off at edges.

Bisque, then cover food surfaces with a tested food-safe clear glaze and glaze-fire. Fire manganese- or copper-bearing slips in a ventilated kiln area — these metals volatilize as fumes during firing.
Leach-test if you used copper/cobalt/manganese under a food surface.
modifiers: White slip on red body (or the reverse) is the classic contrast; combing two wet-trailed colors gives feathered slipware. Add more calcined clay/silica to apply on drier ware. Underglaze-style detail comes from higher stain loads. Wild-clay slips let the body and decoration both come from mapped material.
preservation: Store slip in a lidded container; re-sieve if it settles or smells sour (harmless, just re-blend). Unglazed fired slip is porous and decorative; glazed slip is permanent and food-safe under a sound clear.
beyondInk: The same slip thinned becomes casting or joining slip for handles and attachments. Wild-clay slip doubles as the starting point for a wild-clay slip glaze at higher temperatures.
safety: Dry clay and oxide powders raise fine free silica and toxic metal dust — respirator (P100), mix wet where possible, wet-clean surfaces.
Colorant oxides (cobalt, manganese, copper, chrome) are toxic — wear gloves and a mask; keep out of food-contact glaze layers or leach-test.
Fire manganese- or copper-bearing slips in a ventilated kiln area — these metals volatilize as fumes during firing.
Grind any foraged limestone/silica additions WET and masked.
Unglazed slip is porous and not food-safe — glaze food surfaces with a tested clear.
Occurrence is not permission: confirm rights before digging body or slip clays.

sources.title: Ceramic Arts Network — Seven Fun Ways to Decorate with Slips and Engobes
Digitalfire — Creating a Non-Glaze Ceramic Slip or Engobe
Linda Arbuckle — Slips and Engobes (handout)

name: Silica / Quartz Glass-Former Addition
teaser: The lever that pulls a crazing glaze into fit
hook: Silica is the glass in glass — SiO2, the same building block of quartz crystal, chert, novaculite whetstones, and petrified wood. Alone it needs ~1700 degrees C to melt, so glazes borrow flux to coax it into liquid at kiln temperatures. But the silica you add does one more quiet, crucial job: it lowers the glaze's thermal expansion, pulling a crazing glaze back into compression on the pot. This isn't a recipe so much as the lever that makes every other glaze fit.
yield: A line blend is a handful of test tiles, not a batch of pots — but it can rescue an entire glaze recipe and every future pot glazed with it.

ingredients.amount: Add in 5% steps, typically 20-35% of a base
ingredients.note: Commercial 325-mesh quartz is safest and dissolves better than coarser grades; foraged quartz/chert/petrified wood must be milled extremely fine — a serious silica-dust job.

ingredients.item: A crazing base glaze to correct
ingredients.note: Any glossy glaze that fine-line crazes is the candidate for a silica line blend.
Add alongside silica to keep alumina/silica balanced and glaze glossy rather than dry.

toolsOptional: Ball mill (essential if milling foraged quartz)
Thermal-shock kettle
Dilatometer (lab)
steps: Identify a glaze that crazes (fine cracks) — that means its expansion is too high relative to the clay body.
Mix a line blend adding silica in 5% increments (e.g. +5, +10, +15%) to separate cups of the base.

If foraging quartz, chert, or petrified wood: verify land status and collecting limits first (petrified wood is prohibited in national parks under 36 CFR 2.1 and limited on BLM land under 43 CFR 3622, and may fall under the Paleontological Resources Preservation Act). Then cut and grind WET, then ball-mill to a fine flour. Dry silica milling is one of the most dangerous studio tasks for silicosis — commercial 325-mesh silica is strongly preferred.
Glaze test tiles from each blend and fire to the target cone.

Thermal-shock the fired tiles using the standard IWCT (heat tile to ~300F, plunge into ice water, repeat several cycles) and inspect for craze lines under a loupe. Wear eye protection — tiles can shatter.
Pick the lowest silica addition that eliminates crazing without dulling the surface (too much silica goes stony/under-melted).
If added silica makes the glaze dry, add a little more flux (feldspar/whiting) or a touch of kaolin to rebalance.
Recheck fit and, for food ware, leach-test the final recipe.
modifiers: Silica and kaolin added together in a ~2:1 ratio keep the alumina/silica balance so gloss survives. If you overshoot and the glaze under-melts (matte, pinholed), pull silica back or add flux. Coarser quartz can be used deliberately for micro-texture in art glazes.
preservation: Store silica sealed and dry. Mixed glaze keeps in a lidded bucket. Fired correction is permanent and improves ware longevity by preventing water ingress through craze lines.
beyondInk: Fine milled quartz is also a body filler to open a clay body and reduce warping, and a polishing abrasive (novaculite is literally whetstone quartz). Screened-out coarse quartz makes grog.
safety: Free crystalline silica is the single biggest chronic hazard in a ceramics studio — inhalation causes irreversible silicosis. Use commercial pre-ground silica, work wet, wear a P100 respirator, and wet-clean surfaces.
Never dry-mill foraged quartz, chert, novaculite, or petrified wood without full containment, HEPA, and P100 protection; these are near-pure silica.
Do not dry-sweep glaze dust — it re-suspends respirable silica.

Petrified wood is legally protected in many places — collection is prohibited in national parks, limited on BLM land (43 CFR 3622), and covered by paleontological-resource law; verify land status and limits before collecting any mapped stone.
Correcting fit improves food-safety (no craze-line bacteria harbor) but does not by itself make a colored glaze non-leaching — still leach-test.
Wet-cut mapped stones (agate/chert/petrified wood) to avoid both silica dust and sharp fracture edges.

sources.title: Digitalfire — Silica
Digitalfire — Glaze Crazing troubleshooting
Glazy — Major Oxides in Glazes

name: Talc / Soapstone Matte Glaze
teaser: Magnesium crystals scatter light into a buttery matte
hook: Talc is soapstone ground to powder — the same soft, soapy stone people carve into bowls and stove liners, chemically a magnesium silicate. Load enough of that magnesium into a glaze and, on slow cooling, it grows microscopic crystals that scatter light into a soft, dry, buttery matte you want to touch. It is the mineral behind the best magnesia mattes at both cone 6 and cone 10 — but soapstone and its cousin serpentine can carry asbestos, so this is a wet-and-masked material from the very first cut.

timeline.passive: Firing with a slow controlled cool

yield: A 1000 g batch glazes ~15-20 pieces. Expect to tune the cooling curve; the matte is made as much in the cool as in the recipe.

ingredients.note: Use asbestos-free ceramic-grade talc (e.g., Texas-type talc sold by ceramic suppliers); unverified foraged soapstone must be treated as asbestos-containing and lab-tested (PLM/TEM) before any dry handling, and milled WET.
Alkali flux backbone.
Boron helps the MgO melt cleanly at cone 6 and improves durability/leach resistance. Verify the frit number for your recipe (3124 has alumina, 3134 does not).
Calcium co-flux.
Alumina and suspension.
Glass former and fit control.
Whitens/opacifies the matte.

toolsOptional: Ball mill
Programmable kiln (for slow cool)
Test tiles
steps: If sourcing your own soapstone, treat it as asbestos-containing until a laboratory PLM/TEM analysis proves otherwise — visual inspection cannot rule out asbestiform fibers. When in doubt, buy asbestos-free ceramic-grade talc.
Any cutting/grinding of soapstone must be WET with HEPA extraction and a P100 respirator — dry soapstone dust may release asbestos fibers.

Weigh a generic cone 6 talc magnesia-matte base to be tested on tiles — e.g. talc ~15 / frit 3134 ~20 / nepheline ~30 / EPK ~13 / silica ~10 / whiting ~10, plus opacifier. Treat this as an untested variant and prove it on tiles; do not assume it matches a named tested recipe.
Mix into water to a smooth cream, sieve twice through 80-mesh.
Glaze test tiles evenly — mattes show application flaws.

Fire to cone 6 (or cone 10) and program a slow cool through the crystallization range to deepen the microcrystalline matte; a well-formulated magnesia matte is primarily chemistry-driven and should not turn fully glossy on a normal cool.
Check the surface: it should be silky, not chalky. Chalky/dry means under-fluxed — add frit.
Cutlery-mark and leach-test before food use.
modifiers: Slow cooling deepens the matte; magnesia mattes are chiefly chemistry-driven (high MgO). Trade some talc for dolomite to combine magnesium and calcium for a classic dolomite matte at cone 10. Colorants sit softly in magnesia mattes — cobalt goes lavender/pink, rutile breaks streaky. Keep boron/flux adequate so the matte is durable, not soft.
preservation: Store talc and dry materials sealed. Mixed glaze keeps lidded; re-sieve when settled. Fired matte is permanent; avoid acidic-food storage on any under-fluxed matte.
beyondInk: Talc is also a low-expansion body flux for craze-resistant earthenware and ovenware bodies. Carved soapstone offcuts become bowls, beads, and stove tiles; scrap grinds (WET) into more talc.
safety: Soapstone/steatite and serpentine MAY contain asbestos — unverified stone must be treated as asbestos-containing; 'verified asbestos-free' means lab-tested (PLM/TEM analysis), not visually inspected. Cut and grind WET with HEPA and a P100 respirator, never dry, or use asbestos-free ceramic-grade talc.
All dry glaze handling raises free silica — respirator and wet-clean; silicosis risk.
Under-fluxed magnesia mattes can be soft and leach or cutlery-mark — leach-test before food contact and prefer boron-fluxed bases for dinnerware.
Barium is sometimes used for mattes — avoid barium on food surfaces; this recipe uses MgO instead by design.
Dispose of soapstone grinding slurry as potential asbestos waste per local rules if the source is unverified.

sources.title: Digitalfire — G2934 Matte Glaze Base for Cone 6
Digitalfire — The Right Chemistry for a Cone 6 Magnesia Matte
Ceramic Arts Network — Matte and Specialty Glazes

name: Terra Sigillata Burnish
teaser: A glaze substitute made of pure, mapped clay
hook: Long before glaze, Greek and Roman potters gave their ware a soft glassy shine with nothing but clay refined down to its finest particles — terra sigillata, 'sealed earth,' the gloss on ancient Greek and Roman fine ware. You let a clay-and-water slurry settle, pour off only the cloud of ultrafine platelets floating on top, brush that on in whisper-thin coats, and buff it before it dries. Burnished and fired low, it becomes a lustrous skin — a glaze substitute made of pure, mapped clay.

timeline.passive: 12-48 hours settling; a low bisque-range firing

yield: One jar of settled slurry yields plenty of sig for many pots — a thin coat goes a long way. Only the middle fraction is usable, so expect to discard the grit.

ingredients.item: Ball clay (e.g. OM4) or red clay (Redart) or wild clay
ingredients.note: White clays give ivory sig; iron clays (Redart, wild red clay) give the classic terracotta red.
Roughly 1.5-2.5 parts water per part dry clay depending on the clay; final consistency is set by specific gravity, not the mix ratio.
Keeps fine particles suspended so the coarse grit settles out; soda ash can partly substitute. Do not substitute Calgon water softener — it does not work for terra sig.
Pinnell's red-clay method uses a little soda ash plus sodium silicate.

toolsOptional: Hydrometer
Smooth stone or plastic bag for burnishing
Spray bottle
steps: Weigh clay and water (roughly 1.5-2.5 parts water per part dry clay). Wearing a respirator, add dry clay to water (not the reverse) to limit dust.
Dissolve the deflocculant (sodium silicate, ~0.4% of dry clay; add a little soda ash for red clays) and blend the slurry smooth.
Let it settle undisturbed 12-48 hours. It separates into a bottom grit layer, a middle usable layer, and a top water layer.
Siphon off the top water, then draw off ONLY the fine middle layer — that is the terra sig. Discard the heavy bottom sludge.
Adjust to a thin-milk consistency (hydrometer ~1.10-1.15).
Brush 2-3 whisper-thin coats onto leather-hard or bone-dry ware; over-thick sig flakes.
Before the last coat fully dries, burnish with a soft cloth, plastic bag, or smooth stone for the sheen.
Bisque-fire low — burnished sig keeps its stone-polished sheen best at ~cone 012-010; unburnished/polished sig survives up to ~cone 02-04, but the higher you fire the duller the shine.
modifiers: Firing temperature controls sheen: the lower the fire, the waxier and shinier. Color the sig with 2-10% oxides/stains (more than a glaze needs). Layer different-colored sigs for terra-sigillata painting. Post-fire waxing or a pit/smoke firing deepens the surface. Wild red clay makes a beautifully local terracotta sig. Deflocculant guide: about 0.5% of the dry clay weight, split as 0.25% soda ash + 0.25% sodium silicate. Yield: roughly 2 lb (900 g) of dry clay makes about a pint to a quart of usable sig; siphon only the middle fraction to a specific gravity near 1.15 (checked with a hydrometer).
preservation: Store sig in a sealed jar; it keeps for months but re-check consistency (it keeps settling). Fired sheen is permanent but porous — protect food surfaces with a proper liner glaze or keep sig to decorative/exterior areas.
beyondInk: The discarded coarse settlings return to the reclaim bucket for clay body. Terra sig also makes a fine resist/underglaze medium and a smoke-fire surface for raku and pit firing.
safety: Dry clay is fine free silica — add clay to water, wear a P100 respirator, and wet-clean; silicosis is cumulative and permanent.
Sodium silicate and soda ash are alkaline — wear gloves and avoid eye contact.
Terra sig is NOT a vitrified, food-safe glaze — it is porous; keep it off food-contact surfaces unless sealed with a fired liner.
Oxide colorants (cobalt, manganese, copper) are toxic dusts raw — mask and handle wet.
Verify collecting rights for any wild clay — occurrence is not permission.

sources.title: Ceramic Arts Network — Terra Sigillata 101
Digitalfire — Super-Refined Terra Sigillata
Claybucket — Pete Pinnell's Terra Sigillata

name: Wild-Clay Slip Glaze (Albany style)
teaser: A high-iron wild clay that becomes a glaze on its own
hook: For most of the 1800s and 1900s, American stoneware crocks wore a single dark glaze dug from one clay bank near Albany, New York — a naturally iron- and lime-rich clay that melts almost entirely on its own into a glossy tobacco brown. When that pit closed, potters spent decades reverse-engineering it. You can chase the same thing under your own boots: a high-iron, high-flux wild clay that becomes a glaze at 60-100% of the recipe.

timeline.passive: Days to dry-process and settle, plus firing

yield: A 1000 g batch glazes roughly 15-20 pieces. Expect to spend more time testing than glazing; the reward is a glaze that costs almost nothing and is unmistakably local.

ingredients.note: Dark red-brown clays from lake/glacial deposits melt best. High-clay slip glazes over ~40-50% raw clay shrink, crack, and crawl in the fire — calcine (bisque-roast to ~1000F/540C) half the clay powder and blend roasted:raw to stop crawling.
Add flux if the raw clay won't fully melt at your cone.
Boosts calcium flux for gloss and durability; mapped as ground limestone/marble.
The reliable modern route to bring a stubborn wild slip down to cone 6.
Deepens color toward near-black tenmoku territory.

toolsOptional: Ball mill
Test tiles
Line-blend cups
steps: Confirm you have permission to collect the clay — occurrence on a map is never harvest permission, and many banks are on protected or private land.

Fully dry the clay, then slake whole dry lumps in water — they break down on their own; avoid dry crushing/grinding, which creates fine free-silica dust. If you must crush stubborn material, wear a P100 and wet-clean afterward.
Wet-sieve the slaked slurry through 80-100 mesh to remove grit and organics.

Calcine (bisque-roast to ~1000F/540C) half the clay powder and blend roasted:raw before glazing — high-clay slips over ~40-50% raw clay shrink, crack, and crawl in the fire; this is the standard Alberta/Albany-style fix.
Run a melt test at your target cone: brush the blended slip on a test tile and fire. If it stays dry/matte, it needs added flux.
Line-blend flux additions (feldspar, whiting, or frit) in 5-10% steps until the tile gives a smooth glassy surface.
Check fit on a fired tile — flex and thermal-shock test; look for crazing or shivering.
Once dialed in, glaze ware, keeping feet clean, and fire to the tested cone; ventilate the kiln during bisque/glaze firing of organic- and sulfur-rich wild clays.
Send a dialed-in food-contact recipe for leach testing before using on dinnerware.
modifiers: Higher clay % = darker, drier, more matte; more frit/feldspar = glossier and lower-firing. Add 2-4% titanium or rutile for streaky 'kaki' rust breaks. Layer a thin ash glaze over the top for hare's-fur. Substitute Alberta Slip (the commercial Albany stand-in, 80% slip + 20% Frit 3195 at cone 6) when you can't dig your own.
preservation: Store dry, screened clay in sealed labeled containers to control silica dust. Mixed glaze keeps indefinitely in a lidded bucket; re-sieve after settling. Fired glaze is permanent.
beyondInk: The same wild clay, less refined, can temper a stoneware body or make a slip for decoration. Coarse grit screened out is good pathway fill or grog if re-fired and crushed.
safety: Dry clay processing releases fine free silica — respirator (P100), wet-clean surfaces, never sweep dry; silicosis is permanent and cumulative. Slake whole dry lumps rather than dry-crushing to minimize dust.
Iron-rich wild clays can carry unknown minerals — leach-test before any food-contact use.
Some regional clays contain gypsum/lime lumps that pop or lime-blow in the fire; screen thoroughly.
Ventilate the kiln when firing organic- and sulfur-rich wild clays (burnout fumes/sulfur).
Occurrence is not permission: verify legal collecting rights and leave the bank stable and un-eroded.
Wear gloves; prolonged wet-clay contact dries and cracks skin.

sources.title: Digitalfire — Albany Slip
Digitalfire — Alberta Slip (Albany substitute at cone 6)
Digitalfire — GA6-B Alberta Slip cone 6 base glaze (80:20 Frit 3195)

name: Agate & jasper tumbling
teaser: Patience in a barrel — weeks of grit to glass
hook: A rock tumbler is patience in a barrel: rough agate, jasper, and petrified wood ride for weeks through coarse-to-fine grit and water until abrasion rounds and polishes them to glass. The catch is that these are all silica (SiO2) — dumping a barrel or handling dry grit slurry can free respirable silica dust, so the whole process stays wet and the sludge never goes down the drain.

timeline.active: 1-2 hours of tending across the run

yield: A barrel of polished stones — dozens of small pieces — per multi-week run. Expect some to chip or fail to polish.

ingredients.item: Rough agate, jasper, quartz, or petrified wood
ingredients.note: Best at Mohs 6-8; keep pieces similar in hardness and size.
Each stage in its own sequence; never mix stages.
Optional pre-polish (1200) before the polish step.
Cushions stones and carries slurry in later stages.

toolsRequired: N95 minimum (P100 preferred) respirator for handling dry grit/dried slurry
toolsOptional: Plastic colander
Old toothbrush for cleaning stones
Settling bucket for slurry disposal
steps: Sort rough by hardness and size; load the barrel about two-thirds full with stones plus water and coarse (60/90) grit.
Run the coarse stage ~1 week, then STOP: rinse stones and barrel thoroughly so no coarse grit contaminates the next stage.
Repeat with medium (120/220) grit, then fine (500/600), rinsing completely between every stage.
Run the polish stage with polishing compound and (optionally) cushioning media until the stones gleam.
Handle grit and slurry wet; wear an N95 (P100 preferred) when opening or cleaning anything that could have dried to powder.
Let slurry settle in a bucket and dispose of the solids in the trash — never pour silica sludge down the drain (it clogs and is a dust source when dry).
modifiers: Vibratory tumblers speed the medium-to-polish stages but the coarse shaping stage is still best done in a rotary. Softer stones (obsidian) need gentler, shorter runs and lots of cushioning media. Agate and jasper are the most reliable beginners' stones; petrified wood polishes beautifully but can undercut along growth rings.
preservation: Polished stones need no upkeep. Store grits sealed and labeled by stage; cross-contamination ruins a batch.
beyondInk: Polished stones become jewelry tumbles, cabochon blanks, mosaic and terrarium stone, or worry stones. Failed pieces reload into the next coarse batch.
safety: SILICA DUST: agate, jasper, quartz, and petrified wood are all SiO2. Keep every stage wet; mask (N95 minimum, P100 preferred) when handling dry grit or dried slurry; dust causes silicosis.
Never pour slurry down drains — settle and bin the solids.
Keep the tumbler away from food-prep areas; dedicate strainers and buckets to lapidary.
Petrified wood specifically is limited to small free-use quantities on BLM land and prohibited in national parks (e.g., Petrified Forest NP).
Occurrence is not permission — collect rough only where allowed, leave protected sites and quantities alone.

sources.title: The Essential Rock Tumbling Guide — The Gemstone Lab
5 Tips to Handle Lapidary Minerals Safely — Rock & Gem
Silica (crystalline) — CDC/NIOSH

name: Petrified wood lapidary
teaser: Cut a fossil log as hard as a knife blade
hook: Petrified wood is a fossil that cuts like a gemstone: over millions of years silica-rich groundwater replaced the cellulose cell by cell, so what looks like a log is really solid chalcedony and agate at Mohs ~7. That means you are no longer cutting wood — you are cutting rock as hard as the blade of a pocketknife, and it takes a glassy, mirror polish that shows every growth ring. The catch is the silica: cut and grind it dry and you are making the same dust that gives quarry workers silicosis.
yield: One fist-sized block yields several cabochons or a matched pair of small bookends; a slabbing block yields a stack of slabs for cabs, coasters, and inlay.

ingredients.item: Petrified wood rough (silicified wood / agatized wood)
ingredients.amount: Palm-sized chunk up to a slabbing block
ingredients.note: Denser, agatized pieces polish best; punky or chalky material crumbles.

ingredients.amount: Continuous flow or a full reservoir
ingredients.note: The single most important safety input — it suppresses silica dust and cools the blade.

ingredients.item: Diamond blade + diamond grinding/polishing wheels or wet belts
ingredients.amount: One blade, a grit progression (80 → 220 → 280 → 600 → 1200 → polish)
ingredients.note: Steel diamond wheels for coarse shaping, resin/soft wheels for fine polish.

ingredients.item: Cerium oxide or diamond polishing compound
ingredients.note: For the final mirror on a felt or leather buff.
For holding a cabochon while shaping.

toolsRequired: Trim saw or slab saw with water/oil coolant
Cabbing machine or wet diamond wheels/belts
P100 respirator (for any grinding/polishing where coolant coverage may lapse, and for dry handling/cleanup)
toolsOptional: Tile saw (budget entry for small pieces)
Flat lap for slabs, bookends, and spheres
Dop station / alcohol lamp
Ultrasonic or brush for cleaning slurry off the stone
steps: Source legally — occurrence is not permission. On BLM land, casual personal collection of petrified wood is capped at 25 lb + one piece per day (250 lb/yr) and may not be sold; in National Parks (e.g., Petrified Forest NP) removing any is a federal offense. Buy from a dealer if you are unsure.

Slab it wet: feed the block slowly into a diamond saw running full coolant, never forcing the cut. Let the blade do the work — pushing hard overheats, cracks the stone, and defeats the water's dust control.
Choose and mark your cab or bookend face on a slab, showing the best grain/ring pattern; nip or trim to a rough preform.
Shape the dome/edges on the coarse steel diamond wheel (80, then 220 grit) with water flowing over the wheel the whole time. Keep the stone moving so you don't cut a flat.
Refine through the resin wheels (280, 600, 1200) with a light touch — soft resin compresses into softer bands of the wood if you press, undercutting the polish.
Final polish on felt/leather with cerium oxide or diamond slurry until the surface goes to a glass mirror.
For bookends/blocks: cut a flat base on the saw, grind the show-face flat on a wet flat lap through the grit progression, then polish. Leave the natural bark edge for contrast if the piece has one.
Clean, dry, and — importantly — wet-wipe the whole work area. Never dry-sweep or air-blow silica slurry; let it settle, then wet-clean and dispose of the sludge.
modifiers: Agatized and jasperized wood take a higher polish than opalized or 'punky' wood; the harder and more solidly silicified, the better. Palm wood (with its distinctive rod pattern), Arizona rainbow wood, and Oregon/Washington material each read differently across a slab. Oil coolant in a slab saw cuts cleaner and cooler than water but adds cleanup; water is fine and safer to breathe near.
preservation: The finished stone is permanent and needs no sealing. If a polish ever dulls, take it back through the last two fine wheels. Store the loose slurry/sludge sealed and dispose of it as inert waste — the hazard is only while it is airborne dust, not once it is wet or set.
beyondInk: Byproduct silica sludge is inert once wet and can be dried into a filler. Off-cut slabs make coasters, clock faces, knife-scale liners, and inlay for the wild-clay and woodworking sides of the shop. The same wet-lapidary setup handles the agate, jasper, and chalcedony cabbing on the map.
safety: Crystalline silica is the headline hazard: dry cutting/grinding petrified wood makes respirable silica dust that causes silicosis (irreversible, sometimes fatal). ALWAYS cut and grind WET so no dust goes airborne; wear a P100 respirator for any grinding/polishing where water coverage may lapse (fines can go airborne as coolant evaporates) and for any dry handling or cleanup.
Never dry-sweep, brush, or air-blow the dust or dried slurry — that re-suspends the silica. Let it settle, then wet-wipe and dispose of the sludge.
Wet saws and grinders throw water and grit — wear eye/face protection and keep electrical cords and hands clear of the wet zone (GFCI outlet).
The stone shatters into sharp shards if the blade binds or you force a cut; feed slowly and keep fingers well back of the blade.
Occurrence is not permission: verify collecting rules (BLM caps, NPS total prohibition, private/claimed land) before taking any material.

sources.title: Lapidary Guide: How to Cut and Polish Petrified Wood — Kuno Lapidary
Dangerous Dust: 5 Tips to Handle Lapidary Minerals Safely — Rock & Gem
Can I Keep This? (rock/mineral/fossil collecting rules) — Bureau of Land Management

name: Agate & jasper cabochon
teaser: The oldest cut — a smooth polished dome
hook: A cabochon is the oldest way to cut a stone: no facets, just a smooth polished dome that shows off agate banding, jasper landscapes, or the cell structure of petrified wood. You grind it wet on silicon-carbide or diamond wheels — and wet is not optional, because the water is both coolant and the only thing keeping silica dust out of your lungs.
yield: One cabochon per session as you learn; faster once dopping and doming feel natural.

ingredients.item: Slab of agate, jasper, or petrified wood
ingredients.note: Pre-sawn slab, ~4-6 mm; choose banding/pattern for the dome face.

ingredients.item: Cabochon template + aluminum dop stick and dop wax
ingredients.note: Wax-mount the stone to a stick to hold it against the wheels.
Wheels must run wet — coolant and dust suppression.

toolsRequired: GFCI-protected outlet for the cabbing machine
toolsOptional: Flat lap for the back
Cerium/diamond polish + felt/leather wheel
Magnifier
steps: Choose the pattern on the slab and scribe the outline with a template; trim close to the line with a saw or nippers.
Grind the outline true on a coarse diamond wheel, running water continuously. Plug the cabbing machine into a GFCI-protected outlet since it runs water over an electric motor.
Warm dop wax, mount the stone on a dop stick, and grind the dome — coarse to fine — checking the curve as you go.
Sand up through the fine wheels until all scratches vanish; keep everything wet and wear a respirator around any spray.
Polish on a felt/leather wheel with cerium or diamond compound to a mirror dome.
Release the stone from the dop (freezer or heat), clean off wax, and finish the flat back.
Clean up grinding slurry while still wet (wet-wipe or HEPA vacuum, never dry-sweep) — dried slurry becomes airborne respirable silica.
modifiers: Diamond wheels cut faster and cleaner than silicon-carbide but cost more. Softer or fractured stone needs lighter pressure. Petrified wood and banded agate reward orienting the slab to center the best pattern under the dome.
preservation: Finished cabs need no upkeep; store so domes do not rub. Set into wire or bezel jewelry when ready.
beyondInk: Cab offcuts feed the tumbler; small scraps become inlay or mosaic. Practice cabs make sample sets for teaching lapidary.
safety: SILICA DUST: never grind these stones dry. Water is coolant AND dust control; wear a respirator (P100 preferred over N95) around spray/mist.
Clean up grinding slurry while still wet (wet-wipe or HEPA vacuum, never dry-sweep) — dried slurry becomes airborne respirable silica.
Plug the cabbing machine into a GFCI-protected outlet since it runs water over an electric motor.
Wheels and saws — eye protection and careful finger placement; diamond wheels bite skin.
Dop-wax alcohol lamp is an open flame — keep it clear of solvents.

Petrified wood carries specific federal rules: never from national parks/monuments; BLM lands have free-use weight limits and prohibit commercial collection. Occurrence is not permission — source slabs and rough responsibly, not from protected land.

sources.title: 5 Tips to Handle Lapidary Minerals Safely — Rock & Gem
Lapidary Safety Tips for Cutting & Polishing Rocks — Rock & Gem
Silica (crystalline) — CDC/NIOSH

name: Alabaster carving (translucent)
teaser: Soft gypsum that glows when held to a window
hook: Alabaster is fine-grained gypsum soft enough to shape with a horseshoe rasp, yet polished thin it glows when you hold it to a window — the reason medieval carvers made lamp panels and small saints from it. It has no asbestos risk, but gypsum dust is still an irritant, and worked dry it makes a fine cloud you should not breathe.
yield: One small figure, dish, or lamp panel per block.

ingredients.note: Sold as translucent white, pink, or veined stock; Mohs ~2, noticeably softer than marble (harder 'Egyptian alabaster' is calcite, a different stone).
Brings up the translucent shine; deepens veining.
Use sparingly — too much water pits gypsum. Many carvers sand alabaster damp, not soaking.

toolsOptional: Wooden mallet and flat chisel
0000 steel wool
Small hand saw
steps: Sketch your form. Remove bulk with a rasp — alabaster cuts fast, so go slow near finish lines.
Refine with rifflers. Use a wooden mallet, not steel, if chiseling; a hard blow drives cracks deep below the surface.
Sand damp from 120 up through 600 grit until the surface is glassy; keep dust down with a mask and light misting.
Wipe clean and let the stone dry fully — trapped moisture clouds the polish.
Buff on wax with a soft cloth and polish to a glow. Hold the finished piece to light to check translucency.
Clean the bench wet; do not dry-sweep gypsum dust.
modifiers: Thin walls (under ~1 cm) maximize translucency for lamp shades or votive shields. Pink and honey-veined alabasters carve identically to white. For crisper edges, step up to marble (calcite, Mohs 3) — harder, opaque, and far more weather-tolerant.
preservation: Keep dry and indoors. Re-wax to restore luster. Never use alabaster where it meets water, acid, or weather — gypsum dissolves.
beyondInk: Gypsum offcuts can be calcined (gently heated) into plaster of Paris, then mixed with water for casting molds — the same CaSO4 chemistry potters use for slip molds (a plaster mold, not a glaze ingredient).
safety: GYPSUM DUST: irritating to lungs and eyes. Work damp, wear an N95, and keep ventilation moving.
Use a wooden mallet; steel-chisel blows crack alabaster invisibly and ruin the polish.
Occurrence is not permission — do not collect gypsum from mapped or protected sites.
Wet-clean the workspace; bag dust rather than sweeping.

sources.title: Alabaster — Wikipedia
Safe-to-carve stone: alabaster, limestone, marble — 2Sculpt
Art and Craft Safety Guide — U.S. CPSC

name: Chalk gesso / whiting ground
teaser: The oldest paint ground in the Western tradition
hook: Every gilded icon and gold-leafed frame starts with a humble white ground: calcium carbonate — whiting, chalk, powdered marble or limestone — bound in warm animal glue. Layered onto a panel and burnished, this gesso becomes a stone-smooth, chalk-hard surface that fills the wood grain and gives water-gilded gold a mirror to shine against. It is the oldest paint ground in the Western tradition and the reason a 600-year-old panel is still flat and bright.

timeline.passive: Overnight glue soak + a day to dry between coats

yield: A cup of mixed gesso grounds several small panels; scale the glue:whiting ratio up for larger work.

ingredients.amount: By volume, ~1 part to 1 part glue
ingredients.note: Finely ground marble or limestone dust = the classic 'whiting'; calcium carbonate, non-toxic.

ingredients.amount: 1 part glue to ~15 parts cold water
ingredients.note: The protein binder; soak cold overnight, then warm gently — never boil.

ingredients.item: A rigid wood/MDF panel, sized with warm glue first
ingredients.note: Seal (size) the panel with a coat of the warm glue and let it dry before gessoing.

toolsRequired: Double boiler or glue pot (heat glue without boiling)
toolsOptional: Kitchen scale for repeatable glue strength
Thermometer (keep glue ~55–60°C, below a boil)
Marble/limestone dust hand-levigated from your own stone
Fine wet-or-dry paper for the final polish
steps: Soak hide glue in cold distilled water (about 1:15 by weight) overnight until it swells to a jelly.
Warm the swollen glue in a double boiler until liquid and hot but NOT boiling — boiling degrades the glue's strength.
Size the panel: brush one thin coat of the warm glue over the sealed, sanded panel and let it dry. This stops the gesso from soaking in unevenly.

Make the gesso: sift whiting into the hot glue gradually (roughly equal volumes), letting each addition sink and wet through before stirring, until you have a smooth mix the consistency of light cream. Keep it warm and skim any foam.

Lay the first coat warm and thin, working it into the grain. Let each coat set to touch-dry, then brush the next while the previous is still slightly damp, building 5–8 thin coats (alternate brush direction).
Let it dry fully (a day). It should feel chalk-hard and dead-matte.
Sand/scrape smooth, then burnish with a damp cloth or fine paper to a stone-smooth surface ready for bole and gilding, or for tempera/oil.

Wear a dust mask while sanding cured gesso — the fine dry chalk dust is a nuisance to breathe. If you grind your own stone into whiting, wet-grind/levigate where possible and treat dust from unknown-provenance quarried stone as a potential crystalline-silica (silicosis) hazard — use an N95/P100 respirator for repeated dry grinding.
modifiers: Calcium carbonate (chalk/whiting from marble or limestone) makes the classic Northern European 'chalk gesso'; calcium sulfate (from gypsum/alabaster) makes the Italian 'gesso sottile' — the same method, a slightly different feel and absorbency (standard gilding lore rather than a claim from the cited references). Weaker glue = softer, more absorbent ground (good for burnished water gilding); stronger glue = harder, less absorbent. A little honey or glycerin adds flexibility.
preservation: Store dry whiting sealed against moisture. Prepared liquid gesso is perishable like any glue size — it gels cold, re-melts warm, and grows mold within days at room temperature; refrigerate short-term or make fresh. Finished dry gesso panels are archival if kept from damp and flexing.
beyondInk: The same whiting is a mild polishing powder, a putty and paint filler, and the base for bole/gilding and traditional distemper paint. It ties the map's marble and limestone dust — including your own hand-ground stone waste — into a working ground; the studio ethic of 'one material's remnant becomes another's ingredient' lives here.
safety: Low-toxicity craft: calcium carbonate (chalk/whiting) is not poisonous, and hide glue is a traditional protein binder.

Wear a dust mask when sanding cured gesso — fine dry particulate irritates lungs even when non-toxic. When grinding your own stone into whiting, wet-grind/levigate where possible and treat dust from unknown-provenance quarried stone as a potential crystalline-silica (silicosis) hazard, not merely nuisance dust — use an N95/P100 respirator, not just a loose dust mask, for repeated dry grinding.
Do not boil the hide glue — overheating ruins its binding strength (a quality issue, and a scald risk from hot glue).
Hot glue can burn skin; work over a double boiler and mind splashes.
Prepared gesso spoils and molds within days — don't reuse smelly batches; keep it refrigerated or make fresh.

sources.title: How to Prepare Gesso for Traditional Water Gilding — Gilded Planet
How To Make Gesso — Charles Douglas Gilding Studio
Gesso (traditional chalk/glue ground) — Wikipedia

name: Engraved slate coaster
teaser: Scratch a pale keepsake line into dark stone
hook: Slate splits into flat tiles along its cleavage and scratches pale grey wherever a carbide point drags across the dark surface — the same reason schoolchildren once wrote on slate boards. A rotary tool or a hand scribe turns a coaster blank into a lettered keepsake in an afternoon, but slate is a silica rock, so its fine dust belongs out of your lungs.
yield: One finished coaster per blank; a set of four is an easy afternoon.

ingredients.note: Pre-cut blanks avoid the dustiest cutting step.

ingredients.item: Food-safe mineral oil or stone sealer
ingredients.note: Rinse dust first, then oil to deepen the black and make the pale engraving pop.
For lettering or logos.

toolsRequired: Carbide-tip scribe OR rotary tool with diamond/carbide bit
NIOSH N95 minimum; P100/elastomeric respirator for extended powered engraving or any grinding
toolsOptional: Bench vise or non-slip mat
Fume/dust extractor for powered engraving
Fine sandpaper for edges
steps: Transfer or draw your design onto the slate surface.
Scribe the outline with light passes; slate engraves pale, so shallow is enough. Wear a mask and glasses whenever a powered tool makes dust.

Deepen and shade lines as desired; keep the slate damp or mist the work area during powered engraving (wet method during, not only rinse after), and use a dust extractor or work near ventilation with rotary tools.
Rinse the slate under water or wipe with a damp cloth to clear dust and reveal the finished engraving.
Optional: wipe on food-safe mineral oil to darken the stone and heighten contrast.
Add cork or felt feet to the underside so the coaster does not scratch furniture.
modifiers: Hand scribing gives a soft sketched line; rotary tools give bold controllable strokes. Oil finish maximizes contrast; leave dry for a chalkier look. Larger tiles become trivets or house-number plaques the same way.
preservation: Wash by hand and re-oil occasionally. The engraving never fades; only the oil finish needs refreshing.
