beyondInk: Slate offcuts become plant labels or chalkboard tags. Powdered slate has been used historically as a dark pigment extender — but wet-grind only, wear a P100 respirator, and never dry-sand or dry-grind slate.
safety: SILICA DUST: slate is a silica rock — powered engraving makes respirable dust. Mask up (NIOSH N95 minimum; P100/elastomeric for extended powered engraving or grinding), keep the work damp/misted during cutting, use extraction, and rinse the work wet.
Wear eye protection; carbide points and rotary bits fling chips.
Secure the tile so the tool cannot skid off the edge.
Occurrence is not permission — buy blanks rather than prying slate from mapped ledges.

sources.title: How to Engrave a Slate Coaster (laser alternative method) — xTool
Respirable Crystalline Silica — OSHA
5 Tips to Handle Lapidary Minerals Safely — Rock & Gem

name: Flint-and-steel fire striking
color: Orange sparks, glowing ember on char cloth
teaser: The most beginner-friendly way to use toolstone
hook: Long before matches, fire came from scraping a high-carbon steel down the sharp edge of a stone: the edge shaves off tiny curls of steel, friction ignites them, and a shower of sparks around 800°C or hotter (burning iron particles) rains onto a scrap of char cloth. It is the most beginner-friendly way to use knapping toolstone — no flintknapping skill required, just a sharp edge, which you can dress with a single blow. Chert, flint, quartz, and agate all work; what you actually need is a hard stone with a fresh, acute edge to 'bite' the steel.

timeline.active: 5–20 min to a flame, once practiced
timeline.passive: ~30 min one-time to make char cloth

yield: One striker + one stone + a tin of char cloth is a lifetime fire kit — hundreds to thousands of lights.

ingredients.item: Chert / flint / quartz / agate with a sharp edge
ingredients.note: Any hard (Mohs ~7) silica stone with a fresh acute edge; freshly broken edge sparks best.

ingredients.amount: One C-shaped striker, or an old file/hacksaw blade
ingredients.note: Must be high-carbon (hardened) steel — stainless and mild steel won't spark.
Catches the spark as a glowing coal; make it from 100% cotton in a sealed tin.

ingredients.item: Tinder bundle (dry grass, jute, birch bark, cattail down)
ingredients.note: Receives the glowing char and blows into flame.

toolsRequired: A safe, cleared fire spot or hearth
toolsOptional: Small tin with a lid (to char cloth over coals)
Leather glove for the stone hand
Tinder box to store char cloth dry
Second stone to dress the edge
steps: Make char cloth (once): pack squares of 100% cotton into a small tin with a tiny vent hole, set it in a fire until the smoke stops jetting, then let it cool sealed. Do not open the tin until it is completely cool, or the cloth will flash and burn to ash on air contact. You get black, fragile charred cloth.
Dress a sharp edge on your stone if needed — one sharp tap with a hammerstone or the back of the striker to expose a fresh, acute ridge. This is the whole 'knapping' requirement.
Pinch a piece of char cloth against the top of the sharp stone edge with your thumb, so falling sparks land on it.
Grip the steel and strike down and across the sharp edge at a glancing angle, scraping shavings of steel off. Short, firm, repeated strokes throw the most sparks.
A spark catches the char cloth as a spreading orange glow — protect it from wind.
Fold the glowing char into your tinder bundle, cup it, and blow steadily. The coal grows and the bundle bursts into flame.
Transfer the flaming bundle to your fire lay. Keep water/dirt at hand to fully extinguish when done.
modifiers: Freshly fractured chert/flint sparks best; a smoothed or rounded edge won't bite — re-dress it. English flint, novaculite, jasper, quartz, and agate all work as long as they're hard and sharp. If sparks are weak, the steel may be too soft (test with a known carbon-steel file) or the edge too dull. Amadou (fungus) or true tinder fungus can replace char cloth in the traditional kit.
preservation: Keep char cloth and tinder bone-dry in a sealed tin — damp char won't catch; do not open the tin until fully cool. The stone and steel are effectively permanent; wipe the steel to prevent rust and re-dress the stone edge as it wears smooth.
beyondInk: A gentle, non-destructive gateway to the map's toolstone (chert, flint, novaculite, obsidian) that needs no knapping skill or ARPA-sensitive artifact collecting — you use a broken hand piece, not a worked point. The same char-cloth-and-tinder skill supports the pit-fire and wild-clay cards' fire-starting.
safety: Sharp silica edges cut skin easily — handle the dressed stone carefully and consider a glove on the stone hand.
Wear eye protection when dressing an edge: struck chert throws sharp flakes.

Dressing or breaking chert/flint/quartz/agate releases fine crystalline silica dust — silicosis risk with repeated exposure. Do edge-dressing outdoors, minimize repeated flaking, don't dry-sweep the debris, and consider a dust mask if you shape stone often.
You are making fire — clear a non-flammable area, keep water/dirt at hand, mind wind and dry fuels, and fully extinguish before leaving.
Char cloth production makes smoke — do it outdoors or well ventilated.
Collect toolstone responsibly: use naturally broken hand pieces, and never collect worked stone tools or artifacts (protected under ARPA). Occurrence is not permission.

sources.title: Traditional Firestarting: How to Make Fire with Flint and Steel — Art of Manliness
Flint and Steel Firemaking — Wilderness Awareness School
Fire making with flint and steel (char cloth, technique) — Wikipedia

name: Gypsum Plaster: Molds & Wedging Bats
teaser: The 100:70 recipe behind every studio mold and bat
hook: Every plaster mold, drying bat, and wedging table in a pottery studio is calcined gypsum — the same soft stone as alabaster — ground, cooked to drive off water, and re-hydrated to set rock-hard in minutes. Pottery plaster is mixed by an exact recipe: 100 parts plaster to 70 parts water by weight. Get the ratio right and you get a strong, thirsty mold; get it wrong and it sets soft, weak, or too dense to pull the water out of clay.

timeline.active: 10-15 min to weigh and prep; slurry is pourable for only a few minutes after mixing (30-45 min session)
timeline.passive: 1 hour to set (warm); several days to a week to fully dry before use

yield: The 70:100 ratio makes a predictable volume: ~2.8 lb water + 4 lb plaster fills roughly a small drying bat. Mixed plaster has a short pot life (minutes) — mix only what you can pour before it stiffens.

ingredients.item: Pottery/molding plaster (calcined gypsum; buy USG-type pottery plaster rather than self-calcining alabaster)
ingredients.note: Alabaster is the natural gypsum stone; self-calcining is finicky, so studio pottery plaster is the reliable route.

ingredients.amount: 70 parts by weight (standard 70-73 parts water per 100 plaster, per USG consistency spec)
ingredients.note: Use ~70°F water as the default. Cold water slows the set and increases setting expansion — which can make demolding harder.

toolsRequired: Cottle boards / mold form or bat ring
Mold soap or release for multi-part molds
NIOSH N95 respirator and safety glasses for handling dry plaster (bagged plaster can contain trace crystalline silica)
toolsOptional: Drill mixer (low speed)
Surform/rasp for trimming
Sponge for finishing the working face
steps: Weigh water and plaster to the 70:100 ratio (water:plaster by weight). This ratio sets strength and absorbency, so measure — don't eyeball.

Sift the plaster into the water by hand (plaster into water, never water onto plaster), letting each handful sink until a peak of dry plaster rises just above the surface — that indicates roughly the right ratio.
Let it slake undisturbed for a couple of minutes, then mix slowly and thoroughly, breaking up lumps without whipping in air.
Pour into your soaped mold form or bat ring while the slurry is still fluid (before it thickens); tap/vibrate to release bubbles.
Wait for the set exotherm — the plaster warms as it hardens; demold after it cools back down and is firm (about an hour).
Dry the finished mold/bat fully for several days to a week (a warm, ventilated spot, not so hot it calcines the surface) before putting it to work; wet plaster won't absorb from clay.
modifiers: More plaster (less water) = faster set, harder, less absorbent mold; more water = softer, more absorbent, weaker. Use ~70°F water (up to 100°F) as the default; cold water slows the set and increases setting expansion, which can make demolding harder. Add a splash of vinegar to retard set, or a pinch of salt/potassium sulfate to accelerate — test first.
preservation: Cured, fully dried plaster molds and bats last for hundreds of uses if kept from chipping and from soaking too wet. Never let plaster crumbs get into a clay body — even a tiny lodged bit causes a lime-pop blowout in fired ware. Store dry; re-dry molds between heavy casting sessions.
beyondInk: The same gypsum plaster makes press molds, hump/slump molds, and sprig molds, plus super-absorbent reclaim slabs for reconditioning slop clay. Natural alabaster's other craft life is carving (the alabaster carving card in the mineral set) — a soft, translucent stone.
safety: Never mix or immerse a hand in a large plaster batch that could set around it — plaster sets exothermically and hard, a documented burn/entrapment hazard; keep body parts out of setting plaster.

Dry gypsum/plaster dust irritates lungs and eyes (bagged plaster can contain trace crystalline silica) — wear a NIOSH N95 respirator and safety glasses, don't dry-sweep, and never pour plaster slurry or washout down a drain (it sets and clogs pipes).
Keep plaster out of clay bodies and off wedging clay surfaces used for other work — embedded plaster crumbs cause lime-pop blowouts in fired ware.
Occurrence is not permission — collect mapped alabaster/gypsum only with consent; and this is a studio tool, not a food-contact surface.

sources.title: USG — Plaster Mixing Procedures (100:70 plaster:water)
Linda Arbuckle — Plaster Mixing and Molds (handout)
Ceramic Arts Network — Plaster Mixing 101

name: Lime cycle: limewash & putty
teaser: Rock to caustic putty to stone-on-the-wall again
hook: The lime cycle is the oldest chemistry in building: burn limestone or marble (calcium carbonate) above ~900°C to drive off CO2 and get quicklime, slake that quicklime in water — a violently exothermic reaction that can boil and spit — into a smooth putty, then thin it to a limewash that slowly re-absorbs CO2 from the air and turns back to stone on the wall. It is beautiful and it is genuinely dangerous: quicklime and fresh putty are strongly caustic and will burn skin and eyes.

timeline.active: A day to slake and mix
timeline.passive: Putty matures weeks to months; limewash carbonates over days to weeks

yield: A few pounds of putty makes liters of limewash — enough for a small wall or several test panels.

ingredients.item: Quicklime (calcium oxide) OR mature lime putty
ingredients.note: Most crafters start from purchased quicklime or ready putty rather than burning their own stone. Quicklime is the hazardous step.
Calcining your own requires a lime kiln reaching ~900°C+ — a serious undertaking, not a casual step.

ingredients.amount: Roughly 2-3x lime by volume for slaking
ingredients.note: Always add lime to water in stages, never the reverse, to control the boil.
Only alkali-stable pigments (ochres, oxides) survive lime's high pH.

toolsRequired: Non-reactive slaking tub (steel or heavy-duty HDPE — never aluminum or galvanized; slaking heat can soften thin plastic)
toolsOptional: Sieve for straining putty
Masonry brush for limewash
Sealed maturing bucket
steps: SAFETY FIRST: goggles, face shield, heavy rubber/neoprene gauntlets, respirator, covered skin, and running water nearby before any lime touches water.
Slaking (if starting from quicklime): add quicklime to water in small increments. It will hiss, steam, and can boil — stand back and stir; never lean over the tub.
Let the slaked putty rest under a layer of water; mature it sealed for weeks to months (older putty works better).
For limewash: dilute mature putty with water to a thin-milk consistency (roughly 1 part putty to several parts water), tint with alkali-stable pigment if desired.
Wet the wall, then brush on many thin coats, letting each carbonate before the next. Thin coats bond; thick coats crack.
Keep the fresh work damp and out of direct sun/frost so it carbonates slowly and hard.
modifiers: Thicker putty plus sand becomes lime plaster; on wet plaster, painting with lime-fast pigment is buon fresco. Add casein or linseed for durability tweaks. Pigment load stays low — high-pH lime kills fugitive and many organic colors, so stick to ochres and oxides.
preservation: Mature putty keeps indefinitely under water in a sealed tub. Lime finishes tolerate hairline movement and can be renewed with a fresh coat yearly. Store quicklime absolutely dry.
beyondInk: Lime putty is also a mortar, a fresco ground, a tannery and hide-processing agent, and a soil amendment. Leftover carbonated wash is inert chalk.
safety: CAUSTIC: quicklime and fresh lime are strongly alkaline and cause chemical burns to skin and eyes; slaking is exothermic and can boil and spatter. Full PPE is non-negotiable.
Lime burns can feel painless at first — flush any skin or eye contact with running water for 15+ minutes, remove contaminated clothing, and get medical help for eye exposure.
Always add lime to water in stages — never water to a mass of quicklime.
P100 respirator against lime dust; never breathe quicklime powder.
Home calcining requires a real high-temperature kiln — most should buy quicklime or putty, not burn stone.
Occurrence is not permission — do not quarry limestone or marble from mapped or protected land.

sources.title: NIOSH Pocket Guide to Chemical Hazards — Calcium oxide
Types of Lime — Lancaster Lime Works
Lime: Hydrated, Hydraulic, and Putty — The Mud Home

name: Marble & limestone lettering
teaser: Why Roman inscriptions still read after 2,000 years
hook: Incised V-cut lettering is why Roman inscriptions still read cleanly two thousand years on: a chisel-cut letter catches shadow instead of paint, so it survives long after any pigment weathers off. Soft limestone freestone and fine marble both take the cut. Both are calcite stones; limestone can contain crystalline-silica (chert) impurities, so treat all stone dust as a lung hazard — work damp or wear a P100.

timeline.active: Many hours per line of text

yield: One plaque, address stone, or memorial line. Lettering is slow — budget hours per word.

ingredients.note: Fine, even-grained 'freestone' limestone or statuary marble cuts cleanest. Avoid fossil-packed or crumbly stock.
Draw the letters full-size before any cut.

toolsOptional: Skew chisels
Pencil grid / pounced pattern
Diamond hand pads for cleanup
Hearing protection
steps: Draw the full inscription on paper, then transfer it life-size to the stone with chalk or a pounced pattern.
Cut a central guide line down each stroke, then angle chisel cuts from both sides to meet in a clean V at the bottom of the stroke.
Work with the stone damp or spray-cut to keep silica/calcite dust out of the air; wear a respirator regardless.
Deepen and true each stroke, keeping the V symmetrical so both faces catch light evenly.
Cut serifs and terminals last, then clean the grooves with a fine chisel.
Rinse the stone; wet-clean the bench. Do not dry-sweep the dust.
modifiers: Limestone forgives beginners (softer, quieter cut); marble rewards experience with crisper, longer-lasting edges. Painted or gilded letter interiors add contrast but the incision alone survives weathering. Practice on limestone offcuts before committing to marble.
preservation: Indoor inscriptions last indefinitely. Outdoors, marble outlasts limestone; a breathable stone (not film-forming) sealer slows acid-rain etching.
beyondInk: Clean white marble dust is essentially calcium carbonate (whiting), but chisel dust from limestone may carry silica and bench grit — for food-surface glazes buy commercial whiting; save only clean statuary-marble dust for gesso and glaze filler.
safety: SILICA/CALCITE DUST: both are calcite stones, but limestone can contain crystalline-silica (chert) impurities — treat all stone dust as a lung hazard; cut wet or masked with a P100; chronic stone dust causes lung disease.
Flying chips — always wear impact goggles.
Mallet work is repetitive-strain heavy; pace yourself and support the stone well.
Occurrence is not permission — source slabs from suppliers, not protected outcrops.

sources.title: Safety for Stone Sculptors — Northwest Stone Sculptors Association
Respirable Crystalline Silica — OSHA
Sculpture safety — Princeton EHS

name: Novaculite whetstone finish
teaser: Arkansas stone that walks a blade up to a polish
hook: Arkansas whetstones are novaculite — a dense microcrystalline quartz whose 1-to-5-micron grains act like a fine natural finishing abrasive, from ~1200-grit Soft up to ~4000-grit Translucent, the classic final stone for putting a shaving edge on a blade. Where a coarse stone shapes steel, the hard black or translucent Arkansas refines it to a polish, and truing a slightly dished stone flat is the quiet skill that makes the whole thing work.

timeline.active: 20-60 minutes to hone; longer to true a stone

yield: One well-honed edge per session; a single stone serves for decades.

ingredients.note: Grade by density: Washita/soft is coarser; hard, black, and translucent are finishing stones.
Mineral oil is traditional; the stone can also run with water. Never dry-hone.

ingredients.item: Loose silicon-carbide grit + flat plate (for truing)
ingredients.note: For flattening a dished stone.

toolsOptional: Coarser bench stone for setting the initial bevel
Flat granite/glass plate and SiC grit for truing
Strop and compound for final polish
steps: Set the bevel on a coarser stone first if the edge is dull or damaged — novaculite refines, it does not do heavy metal removal.
Flood the Arkansas stone with oil or water so swarf floats and the pores do not clog.
Hold a consistent bevel angle and draw the edge across the stone in even strokes, alternating sides, wiping swarf as you go.
Progress from soft to hard/black/translucent to walk the edge up to a polish; test on paper or hair as the burr disappears.
Truing: rub a dished stone on a flat plate charged with silicon-carbide grit and water until a pencil grid across the face wears off evenly.
Clean the stone after use — oil it, rub, and wipe the excess so it stays unclogged.
modifiers: Oil versus water is a preference; pick one and stay consistent, since oil-soaked stones resist later water use. Coarser Washita for kitchen knives, hard/translucent for razors and fine tools. A leather strop after the stone chases the last wire edge.
preservation: Clean and lightly oil after each session; store dry and flat. Re-true whenever the face dishes. The stone is effectively permanent.
beyondInk: Fine novaculite is also lapped into pen-tip and instrument-finishing stones. Spent silicon-carbide truing slurry is disposed of, not reused.
safety: Sharp edges — hone away from your body and keep fingers clear of the stroke path.
Keep the stone wet with oil/water — it prevents clogging, and dry truing/lapping in particular can raise respirable crystalline silica dust.
Truing with loose SiC grit is a wet process — rinse, don't sand dry.

Occurrence is not permission — buy graded stones rather than collecting from the Ouachita Mountains outcrops (Benton–Broken Bow Uplift); ancient novaculite quarry sites are protected archaeological resources under ARPA.

sources.title: Novaculite — Wikipedia
Arkansas Sharpening Stone Grits — Best Sharpening Stones
Novaculite (Silica Stone) — Arkansas Geological Survey

name: Ochre pigment from iron clay
teaser: The oldest pigment humans ever used
hook: The oldest pigment humans ever used is iron-stained clay: goethite and hematite tinting a kaolin-and-silica earth into yellows, reds, and browns — the ochres of cave walls and Renaissance flesh tones alike. You can wash a lump of iron-bearing clay into fine pigment by levigation (float off the fines, settle out the grit), then bind it with egg yolk into tempera that cures to a durable film. Roasting yellow ochre drives it toward warm red.

timeline.active: 2-4 hours across a couple of days

yield: A cupful of raw clay yields a few tablespoons of fine pigment — enough for many small paintings.

ingredients.note: Yellow, red, or brown iron-stained clay. Clay is a mapped material — collect only where allowed.
For levigation (repeated wash-and-settle separation).

ingredients.amount: 1 yolk + a little water (about 1:1 yolk to water emulsion, adjusted to a workable consistency)
ingredients.note: For egg tempera; a 1:1 yolk:water emulsion mixed into pigment paste.

toolsRequired: Muller & slab OR mortar and pestle
P100 respirator for dry grinding (dry clay pigment contains respirable crystalline silica)
toolsOptional: Dedicated pan or crucible for roasting (never reused for food)
Palette knife
Gum arabic (alternate binder for watercolor)
steps: Break up the dry clay and soak it in water; stir to a thin slurry.
Levigate: let the coarse sand/grit settle for a minute, pour the still-cloudy fine suspension into a second container, and discard the grit.
Let the fine suspension settle fully (hours to days), then pour off the clear water and dry the pigment paste into a cake.
Grind the dry cake to a fine powder with a muller or mortar — wear a P100 respirator (dry clay pigment contains respirable crystalline silica), or grind wet and dry only the finished cake.

To make red from yellow: roast the dry ochre in a DEDICATED pan/crucible that is never reused for food — never roast wild earths in kitchen cookware — until it shifts from yellow to warm red (hematite), then cool and regrind. Roast in a ventilated space.
Bind: mull the pigment powder with egg yolk (about 1:1 yolk to water) to a smooth paint; or bind with gum arabic for watercolor. Wash hands and tools after handling raw yolk.
modifiers: Different clays give the full ochre range; roasting deepens and reddens any of them. Egg tempera cures hard and matte; gum arabic gives a re-wettable watercolor; linseed oil makes an oil paint. Levigate more times for smoother, more saturated color.
preservation: Dry pigment powder keeps indefinitely in a sealed labeled jar. Egg tempera paint must be mixed fresh (yolk spoils in a day); the dried painting is permanent and lightfast.
beyondInk: The same washed ochre tints limewash, casein milk paint, and gesso, and colors slip for pottery. Coarse grit rejected during levigation returns to the garden.
safety: PIGMENT DUST: dry clay pigment contains respirable crystalline silica (quartz) — chronic exposure causes silicosis. Some earths also carry manganese. Wear a P100 respirator (not just N95) for dry grinding, or grind wet and dry only the finished cake; keep it wet where possible.
Roasting releases fumes/dust — do it in a ventilated space, use a DEDICATED (non-food) roasting vessel, and never assume a wild earth is free of contaminants or roast it in kitchen cookware.
Don't ingest; keep pigment out of food areas and away from children. Wash hands and tools after handling raw egg yolk.
Occurrence is not permission — clay is mapped, but digging it still needs landowner/agency permission.

sources.title: What are Earth Pigments? — Natural Earth Paint
Instructions for Making Egg Tempera — Earth Pigments
Silica (crystalline) — CDC/NIOSH

name: Pipestone (catlinite) — do not carve
teaser: Learn its story; do not carve it
hook: Pipestone — catlinite — is a soft red claystone that Native nations have quarried for ceremonial pipes for as long as three thousand years, at a place so sacred that warring peoples met there in peace. At Pipestone National Monument, quarrying is reserved by federal law for enrolled members of federally recognized tribes. This card exists to say clearly: this is not a hobby material. Learn its story; do not carve it.
yield: Understanding, and a material left respectfully alone.

ingredients.item: No materials — this is an educational card
ingredients.note: Pipestone is culturally restricted. Do not source, quarry, or carve it.

toolsRequired: Willingness to leave a material alone
steps: Understand that pipestone/catlinite is sacred to many tribal nations and central to ceremonial pipe traditions.
Know the law: at Pipestone National Monument, quarrying is reserved for members of federally recognized tribes; twenty-three tribes hold consulting relationships there.
Do not source raw pipestone, quarry, collect, or carve it as a craft material — occurrence on the map is emphatically not permission.

If you want to appreciate it, visit the monument, learn from tribal-authored sources, and support Native carvers directly (purchase of finished work from authorized Native carvers is legal and is the conventional way to support the tradition).
Point others to this card when they mistake pipestone for a soft-stone carving option.
modifiers: None. There is no 'variation' that makes non-tribal quarrying or carving of pipestone appropriate.
preservation: Preserve the knowledge and the restriction, not a made object.
beyondInk: The broader lesson travels to every culturally sensitive or conservation-restricted material on the map: some things are mapped so you know to protect them, not to take them.
safety: CULTURAL RESTRICTION: pipestone is sacred and legally reserved for tribal quarrying at Pipestone NM. Do not quarry, collect, or carve it.
Occurrence is never permission — this card is the clearest example of that rule.
Support and defer to tribal nations and NPS guidance on this material.

sources.title: Pipestone National Monument Cultural Landscape — NPS
Historical Pipestone — Flandreau Santee Sioux Tribe
Pipestone National Monument — NPS

name: Primitive pit-fire / simple updraft kiln
color: Smoke-flashed earth tones, black to blush
teaser: Turn dug clay into pottery in a fire in a hole
hook: The oldest way to turn dug clay into pottery is a fire in a hole. Pit firing dates back over 10,000 years — Jomon, Ancestral Puebloan, and countless others hardened pots at 600–900°C in an open bonfire or a simple clay updraft kiln. It never vitrifies the clay; it sinters it, leaving an open, porous body that survives the very thermal shock of the fire. The reward is a surface no glaze can imitate: carbon blacks, copper blushes, and smoke flashing painted on by the flames themselves.

timeline.passive: Overnight slow cool; days to bone-dry pots first

yield: A single pit or small kiln fires a batch of small-to-medium pots at once; plan for a portion to crack, especially early on.

ingredients.item: Bone-dry, coarse/grogged wild-clay pots (bisque or greenware)
ingredients.amount: As many as fit with space between
ingredients.note: Must be BONE DRY; open, grogged bodies with sand/silica resist thermal shock.
The main fuel — dry only; green wood steams and cracks pots.

ingredients.item: Fine combustibles (straw, sawdust, dung, dry leaves, paper)
ingredients.amount: Layers around and over the pots
ingredients.note: Create the reducing atmosphere and surface color.

ingredients.item: Colorant combustibles (copper carbonate, salt, seaweed, banana skins, coffee grounds)
ingredients.note: Give blushes, flashes, and blacks on the surface; copper compounds and salt release irritating fumes.

toolsRequired: A dug pit or a simple clay/brick updraft kiln
NIOSH N95/P100 respirator for handling dry clay/grog or dry-cleaning fired ware
toolsOptional: Sawdust/dung for a slow smoulder
Kiln bricks or a metal drum shell
Chimney/updraft opening for a simple kiln
A cover (metal sheet) to bank and slow the burn
steps: Fire only where open fires are permitted and safe — check burn bans, get any permit, clear a wide firebreak, and have water/extinguisher on hand. Occurrence of clay is not permission to burn.

Use bone-dry pots made from an open, grogged/sandy wild-clay body. Warm them thoroughly near the fire first (or pre-bisque low) to drive off all moisture — any trapped water flashes to steam and explodes the pot.
Bed the pit: a base layer of sawdust/combustibles, then nestle the warmed pots with space around each, packing fine combustibles (and any colorants) around and between them.
Build the fuel up GRADUALLY — light kindling and let heat rise slowly over the first hour so the pots don't thermal-shock; add wood in stages to a full burn, reaching roughly 600–900°C.
Hold the burn until the pots glow and the wood collapses to coals (several hours). A cover or extra sawdust creates a smoky reducing atmosphere for blacks.
Let it cool SLOWLY, undisturbed, overnight — rushing the cool cracks the ware by thermal shock. Don't pull hot pots into cold air.

Once cold, lift pots out and wet-wipe or rinse off ash rather than dry-brushing, to avoid raising silica/ash dust. Expect some losses — cracks and 'pings' are normal to the method; cracked fired fragments have sharp edges, so handle with gloves and blunt them.
Fully extinguish and drown the ashes before leaving; stir and confirm cold-out.
modifiers: Atmosphere is the palette: an open oxidizing fire gives warm reds/buffs; smothering with sawdust/dung for a reducing smoke gives blacks and greys; copper carbonate and salt flash greens and pinks; seaweed and organic wraps leave line and haze. A simple clay/brick updraft kiln (even built from the same clay as the pots) holds heat better and fires more evenly than a bare pit. Terra sigillata burnished on before firing gives a soft sheen.
preservation: The fired ware is finished but porous — keep decorative pieces dry; don't rely on them for liquids or food unless a proper high-fire/glazed body is used. A post-fire wax or milk/organic burnish can reduce porosity slightly for looks, not food-safety. Store fuel dry; never store combustibles near the firing pit.
beyondInk: This is the firing step that actually completes the wild-clay body cards — dug and processed clay becomes usable pottery here. The grog/sand in the body is the same silica material on the map, and wood ash from the fire is a classic glaze ingredient, feeding the ash-glaze side of the studio and the 'remnant becomes ingredient' loop.
safety: Open-fire hazard: work only where permitted, clear a firebreak, keep water/extinguisher ready, mind wind and dry surroundings, and fully drown/extinguish the ashes before leaving.

Exploding pots: any moisture in the clay flashes to steam and can burst a pot violently, throwing hot shards — use only bone-dry (ideally pre-bisqued) ware, pre-warm it, and heat the fire up slowly. Wear eye protection and keep bystanders back.
Severe burn risk from an open high-temperature fire and hot ware/coals — heavy gloves, eye protection, long tongs, and no reaching over the fire.

Silica dust: dry wild clay, grog, and silica sand contain crystalline silica. Mix and process clay wet, don't dry-sweep, and wear a NIOSH N95/P100 respirator when handling dry clay/grog or dry-cleaning fired ware — repeated exposure causes silicosis.

Smoke and fumes: reducing fires make heavy smoke and carbon monoxide — fire OUTDOORS only, never in an enclosed or poorly ventilated space, and stay upwind. Some added colorants (copper compounds, salt) release irritating fumes.

Pit-fired ware is porous, not vitrified — treat it as decorative; do not assume it is watertight or food-safe. Cracked fired fragments have sharp edges — handle with gloves and blunt them before use/display.

sources.title: Primitive Firing (sintering, thermal shock, temperatures) — Digitalfire
A Pit Firing Color Palette — Ceramic Arts Network
Pit fired pottery (history, technique, temperatures) — Wikipedia

name: Quartz crystal wire-wrap
teaser: A wearable crystal pendant with no grinding at all
hook: Quartz is the one crystal you can turn into a wearable pendant with no grinding at all — a raw hexagonal point, a spool of wire, and a pair of pliers. Wire-wrapping cages the crystal so its natural faces show, which makes it the gentlest possible entry to lapidary: no dust, no wheels, no water. (Faceting the same quartz is a whole other world — that one is wet, dusty, and demands a machine.)
yield: One pendant in an evening; wire and pliers make dozens.

ingredients.item: Quartz crystal point or tumbled stone
ingredients.note: Clear, smoky, amethyst, or citrine quartz all wrap the same way. Clear quartz is fully light-stable; amethyst, smoky, and citrine colors can fade with prolonged direct sun or heat.

ingredients.item: Jewelry wire (copper, brass, or silver-filled), ~20-26 gauge
ingredients.note: Softer wire is easier for a first wrap; thicker gauge for the frame, thinner for binding. Copper wire can temporarily green-stain skin (harmless).

toolsOptional: Nylon-jaw pliers (avoid marring)
Bail-forming pliers
Polishing cloth for the wire
steps: Pick a crystal with a natural taper so the wire cage grips without slipping.
Cut frame wires and form a loop/bail at the top to hang the pendant.
Cradle the crystal in the frame wires, then bind with a finer wrapping wire, crossing over the stone to lock it in.
Tuck and tighten every wire end with chain-nose pliers so no sharp point can scratch skin.
Adjust the cage so the crystal's best faces stay visible; polish the wire with a cloth.
String on a cord or chain.
modifiers: Copper patinas warm and antique (and can temporarily green-stain skin, harmlessly); silver-filled stays bright. Tumbled stones (no points) need a fuller basket-weave cage to hold them. To go further, faceting quartz on a lap wheel is the wet, dust-generating advanced path — mask and water required there, unlike wrapping.
preservation: Re-tighten wraps if they loosen; polish copper when it darkens (or let it patina). The crystal is permanent, but store/display amethyst, smoky, and citrine pendants out of constant direct sunlight, which can fade their color.
beyondInk: Leftover wire becomes ear-wires, ring shanks, or bindings for other stones. Small quartz points make bundled crystal clusters.
safety: Sharp wire ends — tuck them flush so they cannot scratch the wearer.
Eye protection when cutting wire (ends can fly).
Faceting (the advanced variant) IS a silica-dust process — do that wet and masked; plain wrapping is dust-free.
Occurrence is not permission — collect quartz only where legal, and leave protected sites intact.

sources.title: Amethyst & Quartz Gem Care — GIA
Wire Wrapping Basics — Fire Mountain Gems
5 Tips to Handle Lapidary Minerals Safely — Rock & Gem

name: Serpentine carving (asbestos-aware)
color: Deep green to olive, waxy soft-luster
teaser: A hazard-assessment protocol as much as a carving lesson
hook: Serpentine is one of the most seductive carving stones — soft enough to shape with hand rasps, glowing translucent green when oiled, the classic material of Zimbabwean and Chinese figure carving. But serpentinite is the parent rock of chrysotile asbestos, and California soapstone and serpentine are notorious for it. The honest version of this craft is as much a hazard-assessment protocol as a carving lesson: test the stone, work it wet under a HEPA respirator, and — the hardest skill — know when to put a block down and walk away.

timeline.passive: Days for lab asbestos test before you cut

yield: One block, one carving. This is a slow, single-object craft, not a batch process.

ingredients.note: Green carving grade; SOURCE MATTERS — verify low/no-asbestos origin by lab test or do not cut it.

ingredients.amount: Flowing or misting the whole time
ingredients.note: Wet work is non-negotiable — dry serpentine dust may carry asbestos fibers.
Used WET only.
For the final soft luster; deepens the green.

toolsRequired: Wet grinder or angle grinder with water feed + diamond cup
P100 / purple-cartridge respirator rated for asbestos (NIOSH)
Sealed goggles + disposable Tyvek coveralls (bagged as asbestos waste)
HEPA vacuum (never a shop-vac) + wet cleanup supplies
toolsOptional: Asbestos lab test (PLM bulk analysis) on your specific block
Outdoor/downdraft ventilated station
Dop/holding fixture
Diamond hand pads
steps: TEST FIRST. Serpentinite frequently hosts naturally occurring asbestos (chrysotile, plus amphibole). The only way to be sure about a specific block is a PLM bulk asbestos analysis at an accredited lab (a small chip). If it comes back positive for asbestos — walk away; there is no safe hobby workaround.

If you cannot test, treat the stone as asbestos-bearing by default and either don't cut it or use it only under full asbestos-grade controls (many hobbyists rightly choose a different, verified stone instead).
Set up outdoors or in a downdraft/HEPA-ventilated space. Put on the fitted asbestos respirator, sealed goggles, and disposable coveralls. No one else nearby.
Rough the form WET: every cut, grind, and sanding pass runs with water so nothing goes airborne. Never let the stone dry-dust while you work.
Refine the shape with wet rasps and rifflers for detail, keeping the surface wet and re-wetting often.
Wet-sand up the grit progression (120→600→800) for a smooth surface; the stone will start to glow green.
Finish with wax or oil buffed by hand for a soft translucent luster.

Decontaminate: HEPA-vacuum, then wet-wipe every surface, bag the wet sludge, used sandpaper, and disposable coveralls as (potentially) asbestos waste per local rules, and never dry-sweep or blow anything down.
modifiers: Vermont serpentine/soapstone is generally cited as usually asbestos-free while California and some New York material is notorious for it — but 'generally' is not 'this block,' so testing beats geography. Harder serpentine (bowenite) resists scratching and polishes higher; softer massive serpentine carves faster but bruises. Oil vs. wax finish is a look choice: oil reads wetter/greener, wax is more durable.
preservation: The finished, sealed carving is stable and safe to display — the fibers are locked in solid stone, not friable. The hazard lives entirely in the cutting/grinding/sanding dust and the waste sludge. Store no dry serpentine dust anywhere; dispose of process waste as hazardous per local regulation.
beyondInk: Verified asbestos-free serpentine and its cousin soapstone are the same family used for carved bowls, beads, and cabochons; a tested-clean offcut can feed the lapidary bench. The main 'byproduct' of this card is the transferable skill: a wet-work + respirator + HEPA protocol that also protects you when cutting silica stone elsewhere in the shop.
safety: Naturally occurring asbestos is the defining hazard: serpentinite frequently contains chrysotile. Inhaled fibers cause asbestosis, lung cancer, and mesothelioma decades later — there is no safe exposure level.
TEST the specific stone (PLM bulk analysis, accredited lab) before cutting. If positive, do not carve it — walk away. Do not rely on the block 'looking clean.'
Work only WET, under a NIOSH P100/purple-cartridge respirator fit-tested for asbestos, with sealed goggles and disposable coveralls. Keep others away.

Never dry-sweep, brush, or use a regular shop-vac — use HEPA vacuuming plus wet wipe-down. Dispose of sludge, sandpaper, disposable coveralls, and rags as asbestos waste per local rules; do not home-launder potentially contaminated clothing.
Never work a positive/untested block with tumblers, dry lapidary saws, or shared shop equipment that later runs dry — cross-contamination spreads fibers through the shop.
Serpentine also carries ordinary silica/dust hazards; the wet + respirator protocol covers both.
Occurrence is not permission and serpentine barrens host rare endemic plants — gather only permitted float/loose blocks, never disturb the barrens.

sources.title: Sculpture safety (asbestos in serpentine/soapstone/greenstone) — Princeton EHS
Safety for Stone Sculptors (OSHA) — Northwest Stone Sculptors Association
Method 435: Determination of Asbestos Content of Serpentine — California Air Resources Board

name: Soapstone bowl or pendant
teaser: The softest carvable rock on the map
hook: Soapstone is the softest carvable rock on the map — a talc-rich stone at Mohs 1 to 2 that yields to a wood rasp and takes a warm, waxed sheen. That same talc geology is the catch: soapstone and its serpentine cousins can carry veins of asbestos, and both throw free silica when ground. Buy a tested carving block; wet methods control silica, but no technique makes an unverified asbestos-bearing stone safe.
yield: One small bowl, dish, or pendant per block. Beginners should expect the first piece to be a learning study.

ingredients.note: Buy only from suppliers who test and certify their stock asbestos-free — do not rely on the deposit's region.

ingredients.item: Mineral oil or carnauba stone wax
ingredients.note: Deepens color and seals the surface after final sanding. Food-grade mineral oil is fine for food-contact pieces; some commercial stone waxes contain non-food-safe solvents.
Keep the stone and abrasives wet at all times to suppress dust.

toolsRequired: NIOSH-approved half-face respirator with P100/HEPA filters
toolsOptional: Coping saw or hacksaw for roughing
Diamond hand pads
Bench with catch tray
steps: Confirm your stone is certified asbestos-free before touching a tool. If you cannot verify the source, do not carve it at all — wet methods reduce but do not eliminate asbestos fiber release.
Sketch the form on the block. Saw or rasp away bulk stone to the rough shape, working WET so nothing goes airborne.
Refine curves with rifflers and files, keeping the surface wet and rinsing swarf into a catch tray, not down a drain.
Wet-sand progressively from 80 up to 400-600 grit until scratch marks vanish and the surface feels like skin.
Rinse and fully dry the piece. Rub in food-grade mineral oil or buff on stone wax to deepen the color and seal the pores.
Wet-mop the work area and HEPA-vacuum; never dry-sweep stone dust.
modifiers: Green Brazilian or grey Vermont soapstone reads differently under wax. For a pendant, drill the cord hole wet with a diamond bit before final sanding. Serpentine (harder, Mohs 3-4) holds crisper detail but is more likely to host asbestos — treat it with the same caution.
preservation: Re-oil or re-wax once or twice a year to keep the color rich. The stone itself is permanent; only the finish wears.
beyondInk: Offcuts (of verified asbestos-free stock only) become carving practice, pot-lid knobs, or ground (wet) into a talc-heavy powder that potters use as a body flux — but wet-ground powder becomes airborne dust once dry, so mask and keep it wet. Verified-clean soapstone also makes tailor's marking chalk (inherently dry dust — only from tested stock).
safety: ASBESTOS: soapstone and serpentine can contain asbestos fibers. Use tested/certified stock; cut and grind WET; wear a NIOSH half-face respirator with P100/HEPA filters; never dry-grind an unverified stone, and do not carve unverified field stone at all.
SILICA: soapstone also carries free silica. Wet process plus respirator protects against silicosis.
Food use requires verified asbestos-free stone; food-grade mineral oil is a safe finish, but some commercial stone waxes contain non-food-safe solvents.
Gate any talc-powder or tailor's-chalk reuse on verified asbestos-free stock — wet-ground powder becomes airborne dust once dry.
Collect nothing from mapped outcrops without permission — occurrence is not permission.
Wet-mop and HEPA-vacuum; bag debris. Do not create dry dust indoors.

sources.title: Art Studio Safety Policy — University of Chicago EHS
Safety for Stone Sculptors — Northwest Stone Sculptors Association
Respirable Crystalline Silica (1910.1053) — OSHA

name: Black-Walnut Shell Abrasive & Mulch
teaser: Whole-use for the parts you truly can't eat
hook: Black-walnut shells are so hard and angular that industry grinds them into blast media for cleaning jet engines and polishing brass — a softer-than-metal abrasive that scours without scratching. At home, the shells left from a fall nut-crack are the ultimate whole-use afterthought: coarse-ground, they become a gentle scouring grit or a slow-to-rot mulch. This one is explicitly NOT food — it's what to do with the parts you truly can't eat.
yield: A season of nut-cracking yields a jar or two of grit plus a bucket of mulch — modest but genuinely useful, and otherwise pure waste.

ingredients.item: Black walnut (or hickory) SHELLS — the hard inner shell, not the green hull
ingredients.note: Shells are the middle hard layer you crack for the nut. The outer green-to-black HULL contains juglone and is handled differently.
To wash off residual nut oils before grinding.

toolsRequired: Sturdy mortar/grinder or old blender (dedicated, non-food)
toolsOptional: Rock tumbler (shells are a known media)
Bucket for mulch storage
steps: Separate cleanly: keep the hard SHELLS (the part you crack to reach the nut). The green/black outer HULL is a different material — it stains and contains juglone; set hulls aside for dye or disposal, not this.
Rinse shells to remove nut oil and bits, then dry them fully — damp shells mold.

For abrasive: grind dried shells in a dedicated (non-food) grinder or old blender, then sieve to the grit size you want. Wear a dust mask and safety glasses — fine shell dust irritates lungs and angular fragments fly.
Use the medium grit as a gentle hand-scrub for greasy hands or as tumbling media for polishing soft metals and stones (it won't scratch as hard grits do).
For mulch/ground cover: use whole or coarsely-cracked shells as a slow-to-break-down top layer on potted plants or paths — attractive, like a shell mulch.
Keep dogs from chewing shell mulch (moldy walnut material is tremorgenic to dogs), and never use black-walnut material as horse bedding/footing (a known laminitis trigger).
modifiers: Finer grit = gentler polish and hand-scrub; coarser = faster cutting in a tumbler. Hickory shells behave similarly and are less juglone-laden. For mulch, whole shells last longest; cracked pieces knit into a firmer layer. Mix into non-slip additives for painted steps (a documented industrial use).
preservation: Store dry and airtight; dry shell grit and mulch are stable for years. Keep it out of humid spaces to avoid mold on residual nut oils. Label the abrasive as non-food so it's never confused with a pantry item.
beyondInk: The SHELL is the byproduct here; the green HULL is a separate, potent brown dye/ink material (see the walnut-hull ink card). Aged, well-composted shell can eventually amend soil for juglone-tolerant plants. Spent tumbling media can be composted long-term.
safety: Safety first — NOT food: this card is a non-food use. Nothing here is edible; label the abrasive clearly and store away from the pantry.
Tree-nut allergy: shell dust and shell-grit scrubs carry walnut allergen residue — nut-allergic people should avoid grinding or handling the grit.
Wear a dust mask and safety glasses when grinding — fine shell dust is a respiratory irritant and angular fragments fly. Use a dedicated grinder, not a food one.
Juglone: black-walnut material is toxic to many plants; keep it away from juglone-sensitive beds and compost only after long aging (hard shells are among the lowest-juglone tissues).
Pets/livestock: keep dogs from chewing shell mulch (moldy walnut material is tremorgenic to dogs) and never use black-walnut material as horse bedding/footing (laminitis risk).
Don't confuse the hard shell with the juglone-rich outer hull; handle hulls with gloves (they stain skin brown for days). Occurrence is not permission for the mapped trees.

sources.title: Finishing Systems — Walnut Shell Blast Media (industrial abrasive use)
Purdue Extension — Black Walnut Toxicity (HO-193)

name: Calcining gypsum into plaster of Paris
color: Chalky white powder, sets to hard white
teaser: Cook gypsum, add water back, set rock-hard in minutes
hook: Plaster of Paris is just gypsum that has been cooked. Heat raw gypsum or alabaster (calcium sulfate dihydrate) to around 150°C and it gives up three-quarters of its bound water, turning into the hemihydrate powder that — add water back — sets rock-hard in minutes. That single reversible reaction is the whole basis of mold-making and casting, and it closes a satisfying loop with the studio practice of reconstituting alabaster carving dust back into plaster. Note: this works only for GYPSUM alabaster (calcium sulfate) — calcite alabaster (calcium carbonate) will NOT calcine into plaster, and must not be over-heated in an attempt to force it (calcite decomposes to caustic quicklime).
yield: Calcined gypsum loses ~20% of its weight as water, so a batch of raw stone yields somewhat less plaster powder by weight — enough to cast several small molds.

ingredients.item: Raw gypsum / alabaster (calcium sulfate dihydrate, CaSO₄·2H₂O)
ingredients.amount: As much as your vessel holds
ingredients.note: Soft massive gypsum or alabaster carving offcuts/dust; must be GYPSUM alabaster (calcium sulfate dihydrate, soft, Mohs ~2), NOT calcite alabaster (calcium carbonate) — calcite will not convert to plaster at these temperatures and must not be over-heated to force it.

ingredients.amount: An oven, kiln, or steady low fire
ingredients.note: Target ~150°C (300°F); hemihydrate is usable up to ~170-180°C, and true dead-burning to unusable anhydrite sets in well above that (~250°C).

ingredients.amount: ~2 parts plaster to 1 part water by weight, roughly
ingredients.note: Added later, at casting time.

toolsRequired: Mortar/mill to re-grind clumps to powder
toolsOptional: Kitchen scale (repeatable water:plaster ratios)
Airtight storage container with desiccant
Sieve to grade the powder
Fresh gypsum vs. reclaimed alabaster dust for comparison
steps: Confirm the stone is GYPSUM alabaster (calcium sulfate dihydrate), not calcite alabaster (calcium carbonate) — calcite will not make plaster. Reduce the raw gypsum/alabaster to a coarse powder or small chips so heat penetrates evenly (mask + goggles — this makes dust).

Spread it thinly in a heat-safe tray and heat to about 150°C (300°F) in a ventilated space — never use a food-preparation oven for reclaimed/contaminated dust, which can release fumes from resin or binder residues when heated. Hold it there; the mineral is 'giving off' water as vapor. Do not push toward ~250°C or you dead-burn it to unusable anhydrite.
Keep it at temperature until it stops steaming/losing weight (a couple of hours for a real batch) — that signals the dihydrate has converted to hemihydrate.
Cool it, then re-grind any clumps back to a fine, uniform powder. This is your plaster of Paris.
Store immediately in an airtight container — fresh hemihydrate grabs moisture from the air and goes stale.

To cast: sift plaster into water (roughly 2:1 by weight) until an island of dry powder just breaks the surface, let it slake ~1–2 min, mix smooth without whipping in air, pour into your mold. It heats up (exothermic) as it sets in minutes.
Let the cast cure and dry fully before use. Never pour leftover plaster slurry down a drain — it sets in the pipe; let it harden in the bucket and bin it.
Wear a dust mask throughout: dry gypsum/plaster dust is a respiratory irritant, and mixing releases heat.
modifiers: Time and temperature set the grade: ~150°C makes ordinary beta-hemihydrate plaster; well above ~250°C 'dead-burns' it to unusable anhydrite. Pottery-plaster and casting-plaster differ mainly in particle size and water ratio (more water = softer, more absorbent mold; less water = harder). Reclaimed alabaster carving dust calcines the same way, closing the waste loop — but verify it's clean gypsum, not calcite, and not mixed with abrasive or resin.
preservation: Store the finished powder airtight with a desiccant, cool and dry; expect weeks-to-months of usable life before it slowly rehydrates and loses set strength. Cured cast objects are stable indoors but soft and not waterproof — keep them dry; a wax or sealer helps.
beyondInk: Feeds the plaster-mold card directly (slip-casting molds, sprig molds, press molds for the clay bodies) and closes the studio loop of alabaster carving dust reconstituted into plaster. Set plaster is also a modeling/patternmaking material and a carving stone in its own right.
safety: Confirm the stone is gypsum, not calcite: only gypsum alabaster (calcium sulfate) calcines to plaster; calcite alabaster (calcium carbonate) will not, and heating calcite far higher to force it decomposes it to caustic quicklime (CaO).
Hot burn hazard: calcining runs at ~150°C and the material and vessel stay hot — use heat gloves and eye protection.

SETTING PLASTER IS EXOTHERMIC — a large mass (or plaster setting on skin/in a body-part mold) can reach scalding temperatures and cause serious burns. Never cast body parts in plaster; keep pours modest.

Dry gypsum and plaster dust irritate eyes, nose, and lungs — wear a P100/dust mask and goggles when crushing, grinding, and mixing. Calcine in a ventilated space and never use a food-preparation oven for reclaimed/contaminated dust, which can off-gas resin or binder residues.
Never pour plaster slurry down drains — it hardens and clogs pipes. Let waste set solid and dispose in the trash.
Casual specimen collecting only: bulk gypsum is a federal salable mineral, and occurrence is not permission — use offcuts, quarry waste, or purchased stone.

sources.title: Calcium sulfate — gypsum, plaster of Paris, hemihydrate (calcining chemistry) — Wikipedia
Plaster of Paris — Wikipedia
Synthesis, Dehydration, and Rehydration of Calcium Sulfate (Gypsum / Plaster of Paris) — ResearchGate

name: Obsidian & chert knapping intro
teaser: An edge only a few molecules thick
hook: Obsidian fractures along a conchoidal curve into an edge only a few molecules thick — sharper than surgical steel, which is exactly why it cuts you before you feel it. Knapping toolstone (obsidian, chert, flint, jasper) is an ancient craft, but toolstone outcrops are often archaeological sites: collecting artifacts on public land is a federal crime under ARPA, and even natural stone can sit in a protected context.

timeline.active: 1-3 hours for a first flake tool

yield: A few practice flakes and, with patience, one crude point or blade from a first session.

ingredients.item: Knappable toolstone — obsidian, chert/flint, or jasper
ingredients.note: Buy legally sold spalls. Do NOT collect from public or protected land.
The stone rests on your leg pad; the glove protects your holding hand.
Obsidian edges cut before you feel them — essential, not optional.

toolsRequired: Heavy leather leg pad and hand pad
toolsOptional: Abrader stone for platform prep
Tarp to catch flakes
steps: Confirm your stone was legally purchased — obsidian, chert, or jasper spalls from a supplier, never picked up on federal/state land.
Set up outdoors or over a tarp with a leather leg pad; wear eye protection and a respirator (silica dust), plus cut-resistant gloves.
Strike the edge of the core with a hammerstone at the right platform angle to detach a sharp flake — obsidian and chert both fracture conchoidally.
Refine the flake's shape with a billet, then pressure-flake fine notches and edges with an antler/copper tip.
Grind (abrade) platforms between flakes so strikes bite instead of crushing.
Sweep every flake into the tarp — loose obsidian shards are a laceration hazard underfoot. Rinse dust rather than sweeping dry.
modifiers: Obsidian flakes cleanest and sharpest but shatters easily; chert/flint is tougher and more forgiving for beginners; jasper and agate are hardest and most stubborn. Heat-treating some cherts improves flaking, but that is a separate advanced technique.
preservation: Finished tools are stable stone. Store points so their edges do not chip against each other. Never bury or 'age' a piece in a way that could be mistaken for an artifact.
beyondInk: Waste flakes (debitage) make excellent hide-scraping and cutting edges. Broken obsidian is also a striking inlay material — set in resin, never left loose.
safety: LACERATIONS: obsidian edges are microscopically sharp and cut before you feel them. Cut-resistant gloves, controlled strikes, and slow handling are essential.

SILICA DUST: knapping raises respirable silica primarily from chert/flint/jasper (crystalline silica); obsidian dust is amorphous glass, but a respirator is still recommended. Work outdoors/ventilated.
EYE INJURY: flying flakes demand wraparound eye protection at all times.
LEGAL — artifacts: never collect artifacts on federal, tribal, or most state land (ARPA, Antiquities Act, FLPMA).

LEGAL — raw natural toolstone: rules vary by agency; some BLM/NF lands allow casual collection of small amounts, but never collect in parks, monuments, or archaeological contexts. Check local rules; occurrence is never permission — buy your stone when in doubt.
Contain and dispose of flakes; do not leave shards in soil or trails.

sources.title: Archaeological Resources Protection Act 101 — Archaeology Roadshow
Can I Pick Up That Arrowhead? — MeatEater
Silica (crystalline) — CDC/NIOSH
